1988.assessment: Soviet era. No market fiscal data. Soviet budget.

1989.assessment: Soviet era.

1990.assessment: Soviet collapse. Fiscal system collapsing.

1991.assessment: Independence. Fiscal system not functioning. No revenue collection.

1992.assessment: Civil war. Printing money. Hyperinflationary finance.

1993.assessment: Extreme hyperinflation. Seigniorage as fiscal instrument. Fiscal system non-functional.

1994.assessment: Estimated -10% deficit. IMF emergency program. Tax revenues near-zero — no functioning tax system. All spending deficit-financed. Stabilization program beginning.

1995.assessment: Deficit ~-5%. Lari introduced. IMF fiscal stabilization. Tax reform begun. Revenue collection improving from near-zero. Shevardnadze regime.

1996.assessment: Deficit -4%. Improving fiscal position. Revenue collection growing. IMF program on track.

1997.assessment: Deficit -3.9%. Fiscal consolidation continuing. Corruption in tax collection but revenues growing.

1998.assessment: Deficit -4%. Russian financial crisis increased pressure. Lari depreciation boosted some revenues but increased debt servicing. IMF adjustment.

1999.assessment: Larger deficit -5.5%. Russian crisis delayed effects. Revenue shortfall. IMF program under stress. Shevardnadze corruption reducing tax revenues.

2000.assessment: Deficit -4.5%. Improving slightly. Shevardnadze era chronic fiscal weakness — corruption prevented adequate tax collection.

2001.assessment: Deficit -3.8%. Improving. BTC pipeline construction revenue. Tax reform incremental.

2002.assessment: Deficit -4%. Pre-revolution. Shevardnadze era fiscal weakness. Tax evasion endemic.

2003.assessment: Deficit -3.5%. Rose Revolution November 2003. Fiscal position challenged but manageable. Post-revolution fiscal reforms beginning.

2004.assessment: Deficit -2.9% — dramatic improvement. Saakashvili anti-corruption tax reform: doubled revenue collection in first year. Customs modernized. Tax police reformed. Flat tax introduced.

2005.assessment: Deficit -1.5%. Near-balanced. BTC transit revenues. Strong economic growth. Tax compliance improved dramatically. Revenue collection record.

2006.assessment: Deficit -2.5%. Increased military spending. Russia trade dispute impacted some revenues. Gas infrastructure investment. Manageable.

2007.assessment: Deficit -2.3%. Major military buildup spending. State of emergency November 2007. Economic boom raising revenues. Manageable deficit.

2008.assessment: Deficit -6.8% — sharp increase. August 2008 Russia-Georgia War. Military expenditures surge. War damage reconstruction. International aid offset some. Emergency fiscal response.

2009.assessment: Deficit -8.6% — largest post-revolution deficit. Post-war reconstruction + global financial crisis stimulus. IMF $750M emergency loan. Anti-crisis fiscal response.

2010.assessment: Deficit -6.0%. Fiscal consolidation beginning. IMF program support. Recovery underway. Expenditure restructuring.

2011.assessment: Deficit -2.9%. Dramatic fiscal consolidation. Revenues surging with economic growth. Expenditure restructuring. Near pre-war fiscal position.

2012.assessment: Deficit -2.9%. Georgian Dream won elections. Pre-election spending. Fiscal position stable.

2013.assessment: Deficit -2.9%. Georgian Dream consolidating. Social spending increased. EU AA approach. Fiscal position stable at 3% deficit.

2014.assessment: Deficit -3.0%. EU AA+DCFTA signed. Stable fiscal position. Georgian Dream maintaining IMF-compliant fiscal targets.

2015.assessment: Deficit -3.3%. Lari depreciation reduced revenues in GDP-adjusted terms. Regional slowdown. Some fiscal stress but managed.

2016.assessment: Deficit -3.8%. Infrastructure spending. IMF Extended Fund Facility (EFF) program entered 2017. Fiscal consolidation needed.

2017.assessment: Deficit -3.5%. IMF EFF program. Fiscal consolidation underway. Economic growth boosting revenues. EU visa-free.

2018.assessment: Deficit -3.1%. IMF EFF concluded. Fiscal improvement. Tourism revenues boosting tax base.

2019.assessment: Deficit -2.8%. Improving. Pre-COVID economic peak. Tourism revenues high. Russia flight ban reduced some revenue but offset by other growth.

2020.assessment: Deficit -8.9%. Largest deficit since 2009. COVID-19 fiscal response. Tourism revenues near-zero. Social assistance spending. IMF Rapid Credit Facility ($200M). Disputed elections.

2021.assessment: Deficit -7.4%. Recovery spending. Vaccination program costs. COVID continuing. IMF program. Saakashvili arrested.

2022.assessment: Deficit -2.4%. Dramatic improvement. Russian emigre influx massively boosted tax revenues. GDP boom. Lari strengthened. Best fiscal position since revolution.

2023.assessment: Deficit -2.2%. Very good fiscal position. Revenue strong. Foreign agents law protests. EU candidate denied but fiscal position healthy.

2024.assessment: Estimated -2.5%. Disputed elections October 2024. Mass protests. EU suspended accession. Despite political crisis, fiscal position manageable.

2025.assessment: Fiscal outlook uncertain amid political crisis.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR (Soviet Union). No market GDP data. Soviet command economy. Georgia was a relatively prosperous Soviet republic with tea, wine, manganese, tourism.

1989.assessment: Late Soviet. Independence movement growing (April 9, 1989 massacre). Soviet economic system.

1990.assessment: Last Soviet year. Georgia declared sovereignty (1990). Civil conflict beginning (South Ossetia war 1991-92, Abkhazia conflict building). Economy collapsing.

1991.assessment: Georgia declared independence April 9, 1991. Zviad Gamsakhurdia president then ousted December 1991. South Ossetia war. Economic collapse. GDP data unreliable.

1992.assessment: Civil war. Eduard Shevardnadze returned to lead. Abkhazia war 1992-93. Hyperinflation. Economy collapsed. No reliable GDP data.

1993.assessment: Abkhazia war ended (September 1993, Georgia lost Abkhazia). Catastrophic economic collapse. GDP estimated ~$380 PPP comparable. Hyperinflation ~7,500%. Coupon currency collapse. Energy crisis.

1994.assessment: Lowest point. GDP per capita $173 — among world's poorest. Post-civil-war devastation. Coupon (Georgian lari predecessor) collapsed. IMF structural adjustment. Energy infrastructure destroyed. Population fleeing.

1995.assessment: Recovery beginning. Georgian lari introduced (1995). IMF/World Bank programs. GDP $248 — slight improvement. Shevardnadze consolidating power. Inflation brought under control.

1996.assessment: Rapid recovery. GDP $395. Stabilization program working. Growth recovery. Energy sector partly rebuilt. Shevardnadze regime stable.

1997.assessment: Strong recovery. GDP $542. BTC pipeline (Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan) negotiations. Transit economy emerging. Shevardnadze era relative stability.

1998.assessment: Slight slowdown. Russian financial crisis August 1998 impacted. GDP $569. Lari depreciated significantly. Economic shock from Russia.

1999.assessment: Slight decline. Post-Russian crisis adjustment. GDP $549. Shevardnadze regime weakening — corruption increasing. BTC pipeline agreement finalized.

2000.assessment: Recovery. GDP $620. Late Shevardnadze era. Corruption endemic. 'Clientelism democracy' with nepotism. Rose Revolution building civil society.

2001.assessment: Modest growth. GDP $656. Shevardnadze weakening. BTC pipeline construction began. Saakashvili as Tbilisi mayor gaining popularity.

2002.assessment: GDP $733. Pre-Rose Revolution. Shevardnadze election fraud building. Opposition NGOs growing (Kmara! etc.). BTC pipeline advancing.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution year. GDP $840. November 2003 — Shevardnadze ousted by peaceful Rose Revolution (Saakashvili, Zhvania, Burjanadze led). Immediate international integration. GDP growth despite political transition.

2004.assessment: Post-revolution economic surge. GDP $1,018. Saakashvili elected president January 2004. Rapid anti-corruption reforms. Tax revenues doubled. Investment inflows. Economic liberalization.

2005.assessment: Strong growth. GDP $1,268. BTC pipeline opened (May 2005). Energy transit revenues. Economic liberalization (flat tax, deregulation). FDI inflows accelerating. PM Zhvania died (Feb 2005 gas poisoning).

2006.assessment: Strong growth. GDP $1,517. Russia-Georgia trade dispute (Russia banned Georgian wine and mineral water). Gas crisis (pipeline explosion). Economic growth despite Russia pressure.

2007.assessment: Strong growth. GDP $2,131. Saakashvili declared state of emergency (November 2007, Okruashvili protests). Economic boom continued. Major FDI. Heritage Foundation ranked Georgia as reformer.

2008.assessment: August 2008 Russia-Georgia War (5-day war). GDP $2,948 despite war damage. Russia recognized Abkhazia and South Ossetia. Georgia lost ~20% of territory. International aid ($4.5B pledged). GDP still grew due to Q1-Q2 boom and aid.

2009.assessment: GDP $2,526. Post-war recession + global financial crisis. FDI collapsed after war. GDP fell. Recovery aided by international donor program. Saakashvili political challenges (protests March 2009).

2010.assessment: Recovery. GDP $2,777. Economic reform continuing. Free trade agreements. BTC revenue. NATO/EU integration aspirations.

2011.assessment: Strong growth. GDP $3,348. Saakashvili era reforms peaking. 'Georgian Economic Miracle' narrative. Business-friendly rankings improving. Prison abuse scandal (September 2011) shocked society.

2012.assessment: GDP $3,769. October 2012 elections — Georgian Dream (Ivanishvili) won. First peaceful transfer of power in Georgia's post-Soviet history. Economic growth maintained.

2013.assessment: GDP $3,986. Georgian Dream consolidating. Saakashvili left presidency. Margvelashvili elected president. EU Association Agreement negotiations. EITI compliant status achieved.

2014.assessment: GDP $4,429. EU Association Agreement signed (June 2014). DCFTA. Economic growth continuing. Russia-Ukraine war shadow. Georgian Dream governing.

2015.assessment: GDP $3,793 — decline due to lari depreciation and regional spillover from Russia sanctions. Russia-Ukraine war impact on region. Lari lost ~40% of value. Remittances from Russia fell.

2016.assessment: GDP $3,876. Recovery from lari depreciation. EU visa liberalization (2017). Tourism growing. Georgia increasingly a regional financial hub.

2017.assessment: GDP $4,038. EU visa-free travel (March 2017). Strong growth. Tourism and remittances growing. EU DCFTA benefits materializing.

2018.assessment: GDP $4,366. Strong growth. Tourism boom. Financial services. Cryptocurrency hub emerging. Salome Zourabichvili elected president. Georgian Dream dominant.

2019.assessment: GDP $4,757. Strong pre-COVID growth. Tourism peak year. Russia banned direct flights (June 2019 — after protests over Russian MP Gavrilov chairing Georgian parliament). GDP still grew strongly.

2020.assessment: COVID-19 impact. GDP $4,280 (-6.2% real). Tourism collapsed (from ~9M visitors to near-zero). Georgian Dream won disputed elections October 2020. Major political crisis post-election.

2021.assessment: Strong recovery. GDP $4,736 (+10.4% real). Tourism recovering. Saakashvili returned, arrested October 2021 — political crisis. Georgia applied for EU membership 2022.

2022.assessment: GDP $6,050 — major jump. Russia-Ukraine war spillover: Russian emigres (oligarchs, tech workers, draft avoiders) flooded into Georgia — massive remittances and spending. Lari strengthened. Unprecedented GDP boost from Russian migration.

2023.assessment: GDP $7,159. Continued growth driven by Russian emigres, tourism recovery, EU trade. Georgian Dream 'foreign agents' law triggered massive protests (March 2023). EU candidate status denied (Georgia did not meet criteria).

2024.assessment: GDP ~$7,900 estimated. October 2024 disputed elections — Georgian Dream declared winner. Mass protests November-December 2024 (pro-EU demonstrators). EU suspended accession talks. US/EU sanctions on Georgian Dream officials.

2025.assessment: Ongoing political crisis. Mass protests. EU accession suspended. Economic uncertainty.

1988.assessment: Soviet price controls. No market inflation. Suppressed prices.

1989.assessment: Soviet price system. Beginning of liberalization pressures.

1990.assessment: Soviet price system collapsing. Shortages. No reliable market inflation.

1991.assessment: Independence. Price liberalization. Civil war. Hyperinflation beginning. Soviet coupon currency collapsing. Estimated ~131% inflation — start of hyperinflationary spiral.

1992.assessment: Hyperinflation. Abkhazia war. Coupon currency collapsed. Estimated ~887%. Economy devastated.

1993.assessment: Extreme hyperinflation ~3,125%. Abkhazia lost. Economy collapsed. Coupon worthless. Criminal economy. Civil war ending.

1994.assessment: Maximum hyperinflation: 15,606%. Coupon currency completely collapsed. Barter economy. IMF emergency program. Shevardnadze regime barely functioning. Worst economic conditions.

1995.assessment: Rapid disinflation. Lari introduced October 1995. IMF stabilization program working. From 15,000% to 163% — dramatic improvement. Economic stabilization beginning.

1996.assessment: Continued disinflation. 39.4%. Lari stabilizing. IMF program succeeding. Economic normalization.

1997.assessment: Single-digit inflation achieved. 7.1%. Stabilization complete. Economic recovery under way. Shevardnadze era relative stability.

1998.assessment: Low inflation 3.6%. Russian financial crisis (August 1998) impacted lari. Lari depreciated sharply but inflation remained moderate. IMF program adjustment.

1999.assessment: Inflation rose 19.2% — Russian crisis delayed effect. Lari depreciation feeding through. IMF program under stress. Shevardnadze corruption endemic.

2000.assessment: Low inflation 4.1%. Stabilization restored. Late Shevardnadze era. Pre-Rose Revolution. Economic doldrums with corruption.

2001.assessment: Low inflation 4.7%. Stable macroeconomy despite political corruption. BTC pipeline construction begun.

2002.assessment: Moderate inflation 5.7%. Pre-Rose Revolution. Economic stagnation under Shevardnadze.

2003.assessment: Low inflation 4.8%. Rose Revolution November 2003. Economic transition. Saakashvili won presidential election January 2004 — inflation remained stable through transition.

2004.assessment: Moderate inflation 5.7%. Post-revolution economic boom beginning. Tax reform doubled revenues. Rapid growth without major inflationary pressure.

2005.assessment: Rising inflation 8.3%. BTC pipeline opened. Economic boom driving demand. Energy prices rising. Still manageable.

2006.assessment: Inflation 9.2%. Russia trade dispute (wine/mineral water ban). Gas crisis. Economic boom continued. Demand-pull inflation.

2007.assessment: Inflation 9.2%. Boom economy. FDI peak. Food and energy price pressure. Saakashvili state of emergency November 2007.

2008.assessment: Inflation 10.0%. August 2008 Russia-Georgia War. War disrupted supply chains. International aid flood. Inflation elevated but managed.

2009.assessment: Very low inflation 1.7%. Post-war demand collapse. Global financial crisis. FDI collapsed. Deflationary pressure from recession.

2010.assessment: Inflation 7.1%. Recovery from recession. Food price inflation globally. Recovery demand.

2011.assessment: Inflation 8.5%. Strong growth driving demand. Global commodity prices. Prison scandal shocked society.

2012.assessment: Deflation -0.9%. Georgian Dream won elections — economic uncertainty. IMF program adjustment. Commodity price falls.

2013.assessment: Near-deflation -0.5%. Georgian Dream consolidating. Saakashvili transferred presidency. Economic transition period. EU AA negotiations.

2014.assessment: Low inflation 3.1%. EU AA+DCFTA signed. Economic normalization. Growth stable.

2015.assessment: Inflation 4.0%. Lari depreciation (-40%) — import price pressure. Russia-Ukraine war spillover. Higher import inflation.

2016.assessment: Low inflation 2.1%. Lari stabilized. Georgian Dream dominant. EU DCFTA trade growing.

2017.assessment: Moderate inflation 6.0%. EU visa-free. Economic growth. Energy price increase.

2018.assessment: Low inflation 2.6%. Strong growth. Tourism boom. Stable macroeconomics.

2019.assessment: Moderate inflation 4.9%. Strong pre-COVID growth. Russia flight ban. Tourism strong anyway.

2020.assessment: Moderate inflation 5.2% despite COVID recession. Lari depreciated ~15% — import price inflation. COVID aid spending. Disputed elections.

2021.assessment: High inflation 9.6%. Post-COVID demand surge. Global commodity prices. Lari depreciation. Supply chain disruptions. Strong economic recovery paradoxically fueling inflation.

2022.assessment: High inflation 11.9%. Russia-Ukraine war shock — food/energy prices. Russian emigre spending boom paradoxically stimulated inflation. IMF program adjustment.

2023.assessment: Rapid disinflation 2.5%. Monetary tightening worked. Energy prices normalized. Russian emigre boom moderating. Foreign agents law protests.

2024.assessment: Low inflation ~3.0%. Disputed elections October 2024. Mass protests. EU suspended accession. Lari under pressure but inflation contained.

2025.assessment: Inflation outlook uncertain amid political crisis and EU suspension.

1988.assessment: Soviet command economy. Inter-republican trade not market-based. External trade minimal and state-controlled.

1989.assessment: Soviet era. No market trade data.

1990.assessment: Soviet collapse. Trade relationships collapsing.

1991.assessment: Independence. Soviet trade network collapsing. New trade relationships not yet formed.

1992.assessment: Civil war. Trade disrupted. Economy in barter.

1993.assessment: Abkhazia war. Trade minimal. Economy destroyed.

1994.assessment: Estimated ~30% trade/GDP. Trade emerging from post-civil-war collapse. IMF program. Re-establishing trade links. Russia was main trading partner.

1995.assessment: Trade 57.7% of GDP. Rapid trade liberalization post-Soviet. Georgia opened its economy. Lari introduced. IMF program. Turkey, Russia, EU emerging as trade partners.

1996.assessment: Trade 66%. Increasing openness. Transit economy developing. Pipeline transit (early agreements). Russia dominant.

1997.assessment: Trade 72%. Growing openness. BTC pipeline agreed. Trade diversification starting.

1998.assessment: Trade 64%. Russian financial crisis reduced trade. Lari depreciation affecting imports.

1999.assessment: Trade 67%. Recovery from Russian crisis. BTC agreements. Trade routes developing.

2000.assessment: Trade 73%. Shevardnadze era. Trade growing despite corruption. BTC construction advancing.

2001.assessment: Trade 72%. Stable pre-revolution. WTO accession 2000 (Georgia joined WTO June 2000).

2002.assessment: Trade 68%. Pre-revolution slowdown. Corruption affecting trade efficiency.

2003.assessment: Trade 66%. Rose Revolution November 2003. Trade policy liberalization immediately after revolution.

2004.assessment: Trade 70%. Post-revolution liberalization. Saakashvili major tariff reductions. Flat tax. Business environment improved. FDI inflows.

2005.assessment: Trade 83%. BTC pipeline opened — transit revenues. Significant trade expansion. Georgia became a transit hub. Deregulation.

2006.assessment: Trade 95%. Near full openness. Russia banned Georgian wine/mineral water — trade diversification forced. EU, Turkey, China growing as partners. Transit economy.

2007.assessment: Trade 96%. Very open economy. FDI peak. Economic boom. Georgia became one of most open economies in post-Soviet space.

2008.assessment: Trade 95%. Russia-Georgia War — trade disrupted briefly. War damage to some trade infrastructure. International aid inflows distorted GDP. Trade remained high despite war.

2009.assessment: Trade 82%. Post-war recession. FDI collapse. Imports fell sharply. Global crisis.

2010.assessment: Trade 88%. Recovery. Free trade agreement with Turkey (2007). EU PCA. Trade recovery.

2011.assessment: Trade 98%. Strong recovery. EU AA negotiations. Free trade areas developing. Tourism growing.

2012.assessment: Trade 99%. Georgian Dream won elections. Very open economy maintained. FTAs growing.

2013.assessment: Trade 100% of GDP. EU AA negotiations almost complete. DCFTA approaching. Highly open economy. EU, Turkey, Russia, China as main partners.

2014.assessment: Trade 104% of GDP. EU AA+DCFTA signed June 2014. One of world's most open economies. Transit hub. EU becoming dominant trade partner.

2015.assessment: Trade 101%. Slight decline due to lari depreciation and regional slowdown. EU DCFTA benefits starting. Highly open maintained.

2016.assessment: Trade 103%. EU DCFTA deepening. Tourism growing. Transit role.

2017.assessment: Trade 110%. Very high. EU visa-free. Tourism boom. DCFTA fully operational. Among most open economies in the world.

2018.assessment: Trade 116%. Peak openness. Tourism boom. Financial services. China FTA (entered force 2018). Very open.

2019.assessment: Trade 122%. Record openness. Tourism peak year (9M visitors). Russia flight ban but tourism redirected. Highly integrated global economy.

2020.assessment: Trade 91%. COVID collapse. Tourism to near-zero. Global trade disruption. Dramatic fall from 122%.

2021.assessment: Trade 100%. Recovery. Tourism recovering. Strong GDP growth. Saakashvili arrested.

2022.assessment: Trade 113%. Russian emigre influx boosted services trade. Russia-Ukraine war rerouted some trade through Georgia. High openness maintained.

2023.assessment: Trade 116%. High openness. Tourism continued strong. EU trade dominant. Russian re-export trade significant. Foreign agents protests.

2024.assessment: Estimated ~115%. Political crisis (disputed elections, EU suspension). Trade continuing despite political crisis. High openness maintained.

2025.assessment: Trade outlook uncertain amid political crisis and EU suspension.

1988.assessment: Soviet economy. Official unemployment zero. Underemployment concealed.

1989.assessment: Soviet era. Late Soviet. Official unemployment near-zero.

1990.assessment: Soviet collapse. Mass unemployment beginning but unrecorded.

1991.assessment: Independence. Massive hidden unemployment as state enterprises collapsed.

1992.assessment: Civil war. Economy collapsed. Most workers lost formal employment. No data.

1993.assessment: Economy destroyed. Abkhazia lost. Mass unemployment. No formal labor market data.

1994.assessment: Transition economy. Formal unemployment statistics not yet reliable. Estimated very high de facto unemployment ~40%+ but no formal LFS.

1995.assessment: Official registered unemployment ~5.9% — severely understated. Actual de facto unemployment/underemployment estimated at 30-40%. Many in subsistence agriculture or informal sector. Georgian LFS developing.

1996.assessment: Official 8.3%. Still understated. Recovery beginning but formal employment slow to recover.

1997.assessment: Official 10.8%. Georgian statistical office improving LFS coverage. Still understated but improving methodology. Shevardnadze era employment largely in agriculture/informal.

1998.assessment: Official 11.4%. Russian financial crisis impact. Formal employment declining.

1999.assessment: Official 12.6%. High official unemployment. Actual informal/subsistence much higher. Late Shevardnadze stagnation.

2000.assessment: Official 10.3%. Slight improvement. BTC construction creating some jobs. Corruption endemic — public sector jobs clientelistic.

2001.assessment: Official 11.1%. High unemployment. Formal sector weak. Agriculture dominant.

2002.assessment: Official 12.6%. Pre-Rose Revolution. High unemployment contributing to regime change pressure.

2003.assessment: Official 11.5%. Rose Revolution November 2003. Unemployment was key grievance against Shevardnadze.

2004.assessment: Official 12.6%. Post-revolution. Saakashvili reforms created formal sector growth but unemployment statistics slow to improve. Major privatization increased private employment.

2005.assessment: Official 13.8%. Higher measured unemployment — improved LFS methodology capturing more unemployed. BTC opened. Growth not translating to formal employment quickly.

2006.assessment: Official 13.6%. Stable. High growth but structural unemployment persistent. Russia trade dispute disrupted some agricultural employment.

2007.assessment: Official 13.3%. Economic boom creating jobs but formal unemployment persistent. FDI creating service sector employment.

2008.assessment: Official 16.5%. August 2008 Russia-Georgia War. Post-war FDI collapse. War disruption. IDPs from South Ossetia. Employment sharply worsened.

2009.assessment: Official 16.9%. Post-war recession + global crisis. Unemployment peak. International aid helping but formal employment slow.

2010.assessment: Official 21.3%. Major jump — census-based LFS revision. Improved methodology revealing true unemployment. Structural unemployment confirmed very high.

2011.assessment: Official 21.0%. Stable very high structural unemployment. Growth not translating to formal employment. Prison scandal.

2012.assessment: Official 15.0%. Georgian Dream won elections — partial improvement. LFS methodology adjustment. Growth creating some formal jobs.

2013.assessment: Official 14.6%. Slight improvement. Georgian Dream consolidating. EU AA approach.

2014.assessment: Official 12.4%. Improving. EU AA+DCFTA signed. Economic growth. Service sector expanding.

2015.assessment: Official 12.0%. Improving. Lari depreciation hurt some importing businesses. Tourism growing.

2016.assessment: Official 11.8%. Steady improvement. Tourism and service sector growing. EU DCFTA benefits.

2017.assessment: Official 11.6%. Steady improvement. EU visa-free travel. Tourism boom. Financial services growing.

2018.assessment: Official 12.7%. Slight rise. LFS methodology change. Tourism and services strong.

2019.assessment: Official 11.6%. Tourism peak year. Strong service sector. Russia flight ban — some tourism disruption but GDP strong.

2020.assessment: COVID spike. Official 18.5%. Tourism collapsed. Hospitality sector devastated. COVID lockdowns. Disputed elections. Large unemployment surge.

2021.assessment: Official 20.6%. High unemployment despite recovery — lag effects. Saakashvili returned and arrested. Structural unemployment high.

2022.assessment: Official 17.3%. Russian emigre influx created service sector jobs. GDP boom. Unemployment declining from COVID peak.

2023.assessment: Official 16.4%. Continued improvement. Russian emigre boom. Tourism recovery. Foreign agents law protests.

2024.assessment: Estimated ~15.5%. Continued improvement trend. Disputed elections October 2024 — political uncertainty. EU suspended accession. Economic uncertainty.

2025.assessment: Protests continuing. Political crisis. Employment outlook uncertain.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR. Part of Soviet Union. No independent diplomacy.

1989.assessment: Soviet era. Independence movement beginning.

1990.assessment: Soviet Georgia declaring sovereignty. Independent diplomacy beginning.

1991.assessment: Georgia independent April 1991. International recognition slow. Civil war underway. Limited diplomatic integration. UN member July 1992.

1992.assessment: UN member July 1992. Shevardnadze returned. Abkhazia war — diplomacy focused on crisis. OSCE engagement. Limited but growing.

1993.assessment: Abkhazia lost. Georgia joined CIS. IMF/World Bank engagement. UN peacekeeping mission (UNOMIG) in Abkhazia. Bilateral relations expanding.

1994.assessment: Growing integration. IMF program. NATO PfP membership (March 1994). CIS membership. Shevardnadze diplomacy — balancing Russia and West.

1995.assessment: Expanding integration. EU PCA negotiations. NATO PfP. Lari. IMF. BTC pipeline diplomatic support from US. Russia still influential.

1996.assessment: EU PCA signed. NATO PfP active. OSCE in Georgia. Russia-Georgia tensions (Russian bases, Abkhazia).

1997.assessment: GUAM formed (Georgia-Ukraine-Azerbaijan-Moldova) — anti-Russian group within CIS. BTC agreed. US engagement increasing.

1998.assessment: Growing Western orientation. EU PCA in force. NATO PfP. Russian financial crisis. Shevardnadze maintaining balance.

1999.assessment: Council of Europe joined (April 1999). NATO PfP. EU relationship. Shevardnadze era diplomacy. Georgia-Russia tensions (Chechen fighters in Pankisi).

2000.assessment: WTO joined (June 2000). Good diplomatic relations. Shevardnadze era declining governance was concern. EU, US, Council of Europe relations.

2001.assessment: Post-9/11 US military engagement (Georgia Train and Equip 2002). US prioritizing Georgia as anti-terrorism partner. EU PCA. NATO PfP.

2002.assessment: US Georgia Train and Equip Program. NATO PfP deepening. EU PCA. Corruption concerns growing. Pre-Rose Revolution.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution November 2003 — immediate Western acclaim. US/EU enthusiastically supported. Georgia became Western democracy showcase. International integration accelerated.

2004.assessment: Rapid integration. EU SAA preparations. NATO aspiration — NATO invited Georgia to aspirant status. US MCC Compact. Saakashvili Western education used to strengthen ties.

2005.assessment: Strong integration. BTC opened — strategic asset for Western energy security. NATO MAP discussions. US military exercises. EU ENP (European Neighbourhood Policy). George W. Bush visited Tbilisi (May 2005).

2006.assessment: High integration. Russia-Georgia tensions (wine ban, gas dispute). NATO MAP debate. EU Association Agreement discussions. Saakashvili visiting NATO/EU capitals frequently.

2007.assessment: High integration but state of emergency November 2007 raised Western concerns. NATO Bucharest Summit 2008 preparations. EU integration advancing.

2008.assessment: NATO Bucharest Summit (April 2008) — NATO said Georgia 'will become' NATO member but denied MAP (Germany/France blocked). Russia-Georgia War August 2008 — Western countries strongly supported Georgia, froze Russia. EU Extraordinary Summit. $4.5B donor conference. Georgia EU membership aspirations. EU monitored ceasefire.

2009.assessment: Post-war Western solidarity. EU Monitoring Mission (EUMM). EU Eastern Partnership (2009). US Biden 'reset' with Russia created Georgia concern. NATO annual assessment.

2010.assessment: EU SAA negotiations. EUMM continuing. NATO partner status. Recovery. EU EaP Summit Warsaw.

2011.assessment: EU SAA negotiations. Prison abuse scandal raised Western concerns. EU still supporting. EUMM. NATO exercises.

2012.assessment: Georgian Dream won elections — first peaceful transfer of power praised internationally. EU endorsed Georgia's democratic progress. NATO partner. EU Association Agreement approaching.

2013.assessment: EU Association Agreement initialed. EITI compliant. DCFTA negotiations. EU EaP Vilnius Summit. Strong EU integration trajectory. NATO aspirant.

2014.assessment: EU AA+DCFTA signed (June 2014). Ratified September 2014. Major diplomatic milestone. Russia-Ukraine War — Georgia benefiting diplomatically as Western partner. NATO enhanced partner.

2015.assessment: EU visa liberalization negotiations. DCFTA benefits. NATO exercises. Growing EU trade. Western community.

2016.assessment: Visa liberalization approved. NATO Warsaw Summit — 'substantial package' for Georgia. EU EaP. China-Georgia FTA signed.

2017.assessment: EU visa-free travel March 2017. Strong EU integration. NATO aspirant. IMF EFF. Best diplomatic position in Georgia's history.

2018.assessment: Strong integration. China FTA operational. NATO exercises. EU DCFTA growing. Zourabichvili elected president — continuing Western orientation.

2019.assessment: Strong integration. Russia imposed flight ban (June 2019 Gavrilov episode). Georgia diplomatic protests. EU/NATO solidarity. Growing EU trade.

2020.assessment: Disputed elections October 2020 — Western concerns. EU/US mediated political crisis. IMF support. NATO partnership. Integration maintained but election concerns.

2021.assessment: Integration under stress. Saakashvili arrested — diplomatic pressure from West. EU mediation of political crisis (Charles Michel mediation). NATO partner. EU candidate application being prepared.

2022.assessment: Georgia applied for EU candidate status (March 2022). Application delayed — EU gave opinion 'potential candidate' not full candidate. Russia-Ukraine war: Georgia refused to join sanctions, causing tension. US/EU pressing Georgia on Russia.

2023.assessment: EU candidate status DENIED (Georgia did not meet 12 recommendations). Foreign agents law — mass protests. Western criticism of Georgian Dream as authoritarian. Integration trajectory deteriorating. NATO partnership maintained.

2024.assessment: MAJOR DIPLOMATIC CRISIS. October 2024 disputed elections — mass protests. EU SUSPENDED accession talks. US imposed Magnitsky sanctions on Georgian Dream officials (Ivanishvili). NATO expressed concerns. Georgian Dream declared EU accession 'paused until 2028'. Deepest diplomatic crisis since Russia-Georgia War 2008.

2025.assessment: Diplomatic crisis continuing. EU accession suspended. US sanctions continuing. Protests. Uncertain trajectory.

1988.assessment: Soviet era. No foreign direct investment allowed.

1989.assessment: Soviet era.

1990.assessment: Soviet collapse. FDI beginning conceptually but not measurable.

1991.assessment: Independence. Civil war. No FDI possible.

1992.assessment: Civil war. Abkhazia. No FDI.

1993.assessment: Abkhazia war. Economy destroyed. No FDI.

1994.assessment: Minimal FDI ~0.2% of tiny GDP. First foreign investors beginning to explore. IMF program creating some investor interest.

1995.assessment: FDI 1.2% of GDP. Beginning FDI inflows. Stabilization creating investor interest. Pipeline transit opportunities. Lari introduced.

1996.assessment: FDI 2.5%. Growing. BTC pipeline agreements attracting oil companies. BP/Amoco leading BTC consortium.

1997.assessment: FDI 4.6%. BTC pipeline construction investments. Energy sector FDI. Hotels and retail beginning.

1998.assessment: FDI 5.9%. Peak pre-Russian crisis. Pipeline investments. Russian financial crisis dampened some FDI.

1999.assessment: FDI 4.0%. Recovery from Russian crisis. BTC pipeline still attracting. Shevardnadze corruption deterring some FDI.

2000.assessment: FDI 3.8%. Shevardnadze era. Corruption reducing competitiveness. WTO joined. Some energy and telecom FDI.

2001.assessment: FDI 3.6%. Modest level. Pre-revolution. Corruption deterring investment.

2002.assessment: FDI 3.3%. Declining slightly. Shevardnadze era stagnation. Governance concerns.

2003.assessment: FDI 6.4% — jump. Rose Revolution November 2003 driving investor interest. BTC completing. Pre-revolution investor optimism.

2004.assessment: FDI 9.5% — major surge. Post-revolution anti-corruption reforms. International investor confidence. Saakashvili government deregulation. Real estate, energy, banking.

2005.assessment: FDI 11.7%. BTC pipeline opened — energy sector investment. High international confidence in Georgia's reforms. Banking privatizations.

2006.assessment: FDI 15.2% — peak. Very high international investor interest. Heritage Foundation 'reformer of the year'. Russia trade ban forcing diversification but FDI surging from non-Russian sources.

2007.assessment: FDI 18.8% — record high. Economic boom peak. Real estate, banking, telecom, energy. State of emergency did not stop FDI. Largest FDI share in post-Soviet space.

2008.assessment: FDI 12.7%. August 2008 Russia-Georgia War caused FDI collapse in H2. Full-year still OK due to strong H1. War plus global financial crisis dampened outlook.

2009.assessment: FDI 6.1% — sharp decline. Post-war risk premium. Global financial crisis. Investors cautious. International aid partially offset.

2010.assessment: FDI 6.8%. Modest recovery. Georgia maintained open investment policies. IMF program confidence.

2011.assessment: FDI 6.5%. Stable. Economic growth. Prison scandal shocked some investors.

2012.assessment: FDI 5.1%. Georgian Dream won elections — political transition uncertainty reduced FDI. First peaceful transfer of power still created uncertainty.

2013.assessment: FDI 6.6%. Recovery. EU AA approaching. Georgian Dream maintained open investment policies. EITI compliant.

2014.assessment: FDI 7.9%. EU AA+DCFTA signed. Growing investor interest in EU-aligned Georgia. China BRI investments beginning.

2015.assessment: FDI 10.6%. High. DCFTA benefits. Anaklia deep sea port investment beginning. Energy and infrastructure FDI.

2016.assessment: FDI 9.2%. High. EU DCFTA. Tourism and real estate attracting FDI.

2017.assessment: FDI 10.9%. High. EU visa-free. Tourism boom. IMF EFF. China FTA signed. Strong investment climate.

2018.assessment: FDI 10.2%. High. Tourism boom. China FTA operational. Strong fundamentals.

2019.assessment: FDI 7.6%. Moderate. Russia flight ban. Some investment uncertainty. Anaklia port contract cancelled — negative signal for foreign investors.

2020.assessment: FDI 4.6%. COVID collapse. Tourism FDI stopped. Global investment froze. Disputed elections.

2021.assessment: FDI 5.7%. Recovery. Real estate and tourism recovering. Saakashvili arrested creating political uncertainty.

2022.assessment: FDI 8.3%. Russian emigre influx brought capital. New businesses established by Russian relocatees. Real estate boom. Strong recovery.

2023.assessment: FDI 7.5%. Good level. Foreign agents law protests creating some investment uncertainty. EU candidate denied.

2024.assessment: Estimated ~6%. Disputed elections October 2024. Mass protests. EU suspended accession. US/EU sanctions on officials. FDI likely declined due to political crisis.

2025.assessment: FDI uncertain amid political crisis and EU suspension.

1988.assessment: Soviet Georgia. Soviet military forces present throughout.

1989.assessment: Soviet military. April 9, 1989 — Soviet troops massacred protesters in Tbilisi.

1990.assessment: Soviet military still present. Independence movement.

1991.assessment: Soviet/Russian military bases remaining in Georgia after independence. Georgia did not immediately evict Russian forces. Russian forces in Abkhazia as 'peacekeepers'. Russia-supported South Ossetian forces.

1992.assessment: Russian military bases (Batumi, Akhalkalaki, Tbilisi-Vaziani). Abkhazia war — Russian military support for Abkhazian separatists (covert/overt). CIS peacekeeping framework.

1993.assessment: CIS peacekeeping forces (largely Russian) deployed in Abkhazia under Sochi Agreement (July 1993). UNOMIG UN observers. Russian bases in Georgia. Georgia forced to join CIS partially due to Russian pressure.

1994.assessment: Russian peacekeeping forces in Abkhazia (Moscow Agreement). Russian military bases: Gudauta (Abkhazia), Batumi (Adjara), Akhalkalaki (Javakheti). UNOMIG. US began military assistance programs.

1995.assessment: Russian forces: peacekeepers in Abkhazia + South Ossetia, 4 military bases (Gudauta, Batumi, Akhalkalaki, Vaziani). NATO PfP joined — Georgia-NATO mil cooperation beginning. UNOMIG.

1996.assessment: Russian military bases. Peacekeepers in conflict zones. Georgia-Russia negotiations on base withdrawal (very slow). NATO PfP exercises. UNOMIG.

1997.assessment: Russian military bases. BTC pipeline diplomacy — US engaged. NATO PfP. UNOMIG. Russian peacekeepers in conflict zones.

1998.assessment: Russian military bases. NATO PfP. OSCE military mission. Georgia-Russia base withdrawal negotiations. Tensions.

1999.assessment: OSCE Istanbul Summit (1999) — Russia agreed to withdraw from Gudauta and Vaziani bases. Georgia demanding Russian military withdrawal. NATO PfP. UNOMIG. Council of Europe.

2000.assessment: Russian bases partial withdrawal. Vaziani base closed. Gudauta partial withdrawal (Russia kept some forces despite agreement). Batumi and Akhalkalaki still open.

2001.assessment: US Georgia Train and Equip Program (2002 — starting negotiations). Russian peacekeepers. Pankisi Gorge — Chechen fighters. Russia threatened military action in Georgia. UNOMIG.

2002.assessment: US Georgia Train and Equip Program deployed (US Special Forces training Georgian military). 1,000+ US troops training. Russian peacekeepers in Abkhazia/S.Ossetia. Russian bases Batumi, Akhalkalaki. Competing military presences.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution November 2003. US military advisers present. Russian peacekeepers in Abkhazia/S.Ossetia. Russian bases Batumi and Akhalkalaki (still open). UNOMIG. Georgia demanding Russian withdrawal.

2004.assessment: Post-revolution. US military engagement accelerated. Georgian military reform. Russian base withdrawal negotiations intensifying. Saakashvili demanded Russian withdrawal. NATO aspirant.

2005.assessment: Georgian-Russian agreement: Russian bases Batumi and Akhalkalaki to close by 2008. US military advisers. NATO exercises. UNOMIG. Georgian military modernization with US/NATO assistance.

2006.assessment: Russian base withdrawal beginning. Batumi and Akhalkalaki withdrawal starting. Georgian military buildup with US/NATO assistance. Russian peacekeepers still in Abkhazia/S.Ossetia. UNOMIG.

2007.assessment: Russian base withdrawal completing. Batumi closed, Akhalkalaki closing. Georgia significant military buildup — $800M+ military spending. US military exercises. NATO aspirant. UNOMIG.

2008.assessment: August 2008 Russia-Georgia War. Russian forces invaded Georgia, advanced toward Tbilisi, occupied South Ossetia, Gori, parts of western Georgia. NATO Bucharest: Georgia 'will join' but no MAP. Post-war: Russia kept forces in Abkhazia and South Ossetia (recognized as 'independent'). Russian forces at Tskhinvali 40km from Tbilisi. EUMM deployed (no military). UNOMIG ended.

2009.assessment: Russian forces permanent in Abkhazia and South Ossetia (~7,000+ troops at Gudauta base). 'Borderization' — creeping annexation of Georgian territory. EUMM monitoring ceasefire lines. No NATO/Western forces in Georgia. US aid.

2010.assessment: Russian forces permanent in Abkhazia (Gudauta base) and South Ossetia. EUMM on ceasefire lines. NATO exercises with Georgian forces. Georgia-NATO deepened partnership.

2011.assessment: Russian forces permanent in occupied territories. EUMM. NATO exercises. Georgia contributed troops to ISAF Afghanistan. Georgian military reform continuing.

2012.assessment: Russian forces permanent. EUMM. Georgian Dream — Georgian military doctrine shift away from military recapture to peaceful reintegration. NATO exercises continuing.

2013.assessment: Russian forces permanent in Abkhazia and South Ossetia. EUMM. NATO partnership advancing. EU AA approaching. Georgia contributing to various NATO missions.

2014.assessment: Russia-Ukraine war — NATO substantially increased Georgia partnership. EU AA+DCFTA signed. NATO-Georgia enhanced partnership. EUMM. Russian forces in occupied territories. NATO Wales Summit — 'Substantial Package'.

2015.assessment: Russian forces permanent. NATO-Georgia Joint Training Centre opened. EUMM. Georgian military trained by NATO standards. DCFTA. NATO aspirant.

2016.assessment: NATO Warsaw Summit — 'Substantial Package' confirmed. Georgia Contributing to Resolute Support (Afghanistan). EUMM. Russian forces permanent.

2017.assessment: NATO exercises. EUMM. Russian forces permanent. NATO aspirant but MAP still not granted. EU visa-free.

2018.assessment: NATO exercises. EUMM. Russian forces permanent. NATO Brussels Summit — continued substantial package. Georgian military NATO-compatible.

2019.assessment: NATO exercises. EUMM. Russian forces permanent and creeping borderization continuing. Gavrilov episode (Russian MP at Georgian parliament). Georgia-NATO partnership strong.

2020.assessment: NATO exercises. EUMM. Russian forces permanent. COVID reduced exercise tempo. Disputed elections October 2020.

2021.assessment: NATO exercises. EUMM. Russian forces permanent. NATO Brussels (June 2021) — Georgia 'will be a member'. Georgian military NATO-standard. Saakashvili arrested.

2022.assessment: Russia-Ukraine war — NATO increased focus on Georgia. EUMM. Russian forces permanent. Georgia applied for EU membership. Georgian military NATO-standard but no Article 5 coverage. NATO Madrid Summit — continued strong support.

2023.assessment: NATO exercises. EUMM. Russian forces permanent. NATO Vilnius Summit — no MAP for Georgia but continued support. Foreign agents protests. EU candidate denied.

2024.assessment: NATO exercises reduced due to Georgia-NATO tensions. EUMM continuing. Russian forces permanent. EU accession suspended. Georgian Dream moving away from NATO/EU. NATO Washington Summit — concerns expressed.

2025.assessment: Russian forces permanent in occupied territories. EUMM. NATO-Georgia relationship under strain. Protests. Uncertain trajectory.

1988.assessment: Soviet era. No refugee flows in UNHCR terms.

1989.assessment: Soviet era.

1990.assessment: Soviet collapse. South Ossetia conflict beginning. Early IDP flows.

1991.assessment: South Ossetia war 1991-92. Mass population displacement. Early IDP crisis but UNHCR data not established.

1992.assessment: Abkhazia war beginning (August 1992). Mass displacement. IDPs from Abkhazia and South Ossetia.

1993.assessment: Abkhazia war ended (September 1993). ~250,000 ethnic Georgians fled Abkhazia (largest IDP crisis in post-Soviet space at the time). Plus ~10K from South Ossetia. Total ~260K IDPs in Georgia — major humanitarian crisis.

1994.assessment: Georgia hosting ~280,000 IDPs — Abkhazia and South Ossetia displaced persons. Largest IDP crisis per capita in post-Soviet space. Shevardnadze government unable to support them. International humanitarian aid.

1995.assessment: ~280,000 IDPs still in Georgia. No progress on return to Abkhazia or South Ossetia. IDPs in deteriorating conditions in former Soviet sanatoriums, hotels, collective housing.

1996.assessment: ~278,000 IDPs. Protracted displacement. Negotiations ongoing (CIS peacekeeping in Abkhazia). No significant return.

1997.assessment: ~275,000 IDPs. Protracted. Some small returns to Gali district in Abkhazia. Mainly still displaced.

1998.assessment: ~272,000 IDPs. Stable. Russian crisis impact on Georgia reduced capacity to support.

1999.assessment: ~270,000 IDPs. Protracted. Second Chechen war — some additional asylum seekers from Chechnya. Georgia a transit/refuge.

2000.assessment: ~268,000 IDPs. Still protracted. No negotiated solution for Abkhazia or South Ossetia.

2001.assessment: ~265,000 IDPs. Slow attrition through natural means. No return.

2002.assessment: ~263,000 IDPs. Pre-Rose Revolution. IDP housing reform beginning.

2003.assessment: ~262,000 IDPs. Rose Revolution November 2003. New government committed to IDP housing reform.

2004.assessment: ~260,000 IDPs. Post-revolution. Saakashvili government began IDP housing reform. Still massive displacement. Abkhazia/S.Ossetia unresolved.

2005.assessment: ~257,000 IDPs. Saakashvili government built IDP housing. Partial rehousing. BTC opened. Economic growth creating some IDP employment.

2006.assessment: ~254,000 IDPs. Housing program continuing. Russia banned Georgian goods — economic pressure on IDPs.

2007.assessment: ~252,000 IDPs. IDP housing program partial progress. Economic boom improving IDP conditions. South Ossetia status negotiations.

2008.assessment: ~287,000 IDPs — sharp increase. August 2008 Russia-Georgia War created ~26,000 NEW IDPs from South Ossetia (ethnic Georgians fled South Ossetia and adjacent villages). Existing IDPs from Abkhazia maintained. Major new humanitarian crisis.

2009.assessment: ~272,000 IDPs. Post-war. Some new IDPs returned to areas under Georgian control. But ~26K permanently displaced. Total remains elevated.

2010.assessment: ~270,000 IDPs. Stable post-war. Georgian Dream government IDP strategy. No solution for Abkhazia/South Ossetia.

2011.assessment: ~260,000 IDPs. IDP housing program continuing. Georgian Dream approach shifting from military return to integration. Still large protracted displacement.

2012.assessment: ~258,000 IDPs. Georgian Dream won elections. IDP policy shift — less confrontational with Russia, focus on integration/housing.

2013.assessment: ~255,000 IDPs. IDP law reformed. EU AA approaching. IDP integration into Georgian society advancing.

2014.assessment: ~252,000 IDPs. EU AA signed. IDP housing program nearing completion for some categories.

2015.assessment: ~248,000 IDPs. Gradual decline through integration. Syria crisis — some Syrians transiting Georgia.

2016.assessment: ~245,000 IDPs. Gradual decline. IDP integration improving.

2017.assessment: ~240,000 IDPs. Still one of largest IDP populations in Europe/Caucasus per capita. No political solution in sight.

2018.assessment: ~238,000 IDPs. Stable decline. Georgian Dream approach: integration rather than military return.

2019.assessment: ~235,000 IDPs. Continuing decline. Russia flight ban. Russia-Georgia tensions on Abkhazia/South Ossetia.

2020.assessment: ~232,000 IDPs. COVID reduced some IDP mobility. Political crisis (disputed elections). Abkhazia/South Ossetia status unchanged.

2021.assessment: ~229,000 IDPs. Gradual decline. Integration advancing. Some IDPs now second or third generation in displacement.

2022.assessment: ~226,000 IDPs. Russia-Ukraine war — Russian emigres arrived in Georgia but not refugees (economic/political migrants). Abkhazia/S.Ossetia IDPs continuing protracted displacement.

2023.assessment: ~222,000 IDPs. Foreign agents law protests. No progress on Abkhazia/South Ossetia.

2024.assessment: ~219,000 IDPs. Disputed elections October 2024 — political crisis. IDP situation unchanged. Protracted. EU suspended accession.

2025.assessment: IDP situation ongoing. Protests. Political crisis. No political solution for Abkhazia/South Ossetia.

1988.assessment: Soviet Georgia. No international sanctions.

1989.assessment: Soviet era. No sanctions on Georgia.

1990.assessment: Soviet collapse. No sanctions on Georgia.

1991.assessment: Georgia independent. International recognition. No sanctions. International community engaging with new state.

1992.assessment: No sanctions on Georgia. Civil war. UN engagement. Georgia joined UN July 1992.

1993.assessment: No sanctions. Abkhazia war — international community did not sanction Georgia. IMF/World Bank engagement.

1994.assessment: No sanctions. IMF emergency program. International aid. Georgia integrated into international financial system.

1995.assessment: No sanctions. Lari introduced. IMF program. International integration.

1996.assessment: No sanctions. EU PCA (Partnership and Cooperation Agreement) signed. OSCE in Georgia.

1997.assessment: No sanctions. BTC negotiations. NATO PfP. NATO/EU orientation beginning.

1998.assessment: No sanctions. Russian financial crisis impacted Georgia. Western aid.

1999.assessment: No sanctions. Georgia joined Council of Europe 1999. NATO/EU aspirations.

2000.assessment: No sanctions. Georgia joined WTO June 2000. International integration advancing.

2001.assessment: No sanctions. Post-9/11 US increased military presence in Georgia (Pankisi Gorge Chechen fighters issue).

2002.assessment: No sanctions. US Georgia Train and Equip program. Anti-corruption pressure growing.

2003.assessment: No sanctions. Rose Revolution November 2003. International community enthusiastically welcomed. US/EU support for revolution.

2004.assessment: No sanctions. Post-revolution Western engagement maximum. Saakashvili era international darling.

2005.assessment: No sanctions. BTC opened. US Compact funding (MCC). Strong international position.

2006.assessment: No Western/international sanctions on Georgia. Russia's unofficial sanctions on Georgia (wine, mineral water ban, postal/transport blockade) — Georgia was the target of Russian pressure, not sanctioned by West. US/EU supporting Georgia.

2007.assessment: No sanctions. State of emergency November 2007 — Western criticism but no sanctions. Georgia still western-aligned.

2008.assessment: No sanctions on Georgia from international community. Russia-Georgia War August 2008 — Western community sanctioned RUSSIA (not Georgia). $4.5B donor conference. Georgia received massive aid. Russia suspended WTO membership negotiations with Georgia.

2009.assessment: No sanctions. Post-war recovery. International aid. EU Monitoring Mission (EUMM) in Georgia.

2010.assessment: No sanctions. Recovery. EU SAA negotiations. Western alignment maintained.

2011.assessment: No sanctions. Prison abuse scandal — Western criticism but no sanctions. Reform credentials questioned.

2012.assessment: No sanctions. Georgian Dream won elections. Peaceful transfer. International approval.

2013.assessment: No sanctions. EU AA approaching. Good relations with EU/US. EITI compliant.

2014.assessment: No sanctions. EU AA+DCFTA signed. Georgia benefiting as Russia sanctioned by West. Strong EU/US relations.

2015.assessment: No sanctions. DCFTA operational. Strong Western support. Georgia navigating between NATO aspiration and Russian pressure.

2016.assessment: No sanctions. Strong international position. Visa liberalization with EU approaching.

2017.assessment: No sanctions. EU visa-free travel March 2017. NATO aspirant. Strong Western integration.

2018.assessment: No sanctions. Positive Western relations. China FTA. IMF EFF concluded.

2019.assessment: No sanctions. Russia banned flights (June 2019 — Gavrilov episode) — Georgia was target not sanctioned. Western support.

2020.assessment: No sanctions. Disputed elections — Western criticism but no sanctions. US/EU expressed concerns about elections. IMF support.

2021.assessment: No sanctions. Saakashvili arrested — Western criticism. Georgia-EU tensions over IDP law reversals. Still no sanctions.

2022.assessment: No sanctions on Georgia. Russia-Ukraine war — Georgia did not join Western sanctions on Russia. Western pressure but no sanctions on Georgia itself. EU candidate application (March 2022) — delayed.

2023.assessment: No sanctions on Georgia. Foreign agents law protests. EU candidate status denied (Georgia did not meet criteria). US/EU concern but no sanctions. Georgian Dream accused of pro-Russian drift.

2024.assessment: TARGETED SANCTIONS BEGIN. US (Magnitsky-type) sanctions on specific Georgian Dream officials including GD chairman Ivanishvili. October 2024 disputed elections. Mass protests. EU suspended accession process. US imposed visa bans on Georgian officials. First real sanctions on Georgia.

2025.assessment: Sanctions continuing and possibly expanding. Political crisis ongoing. EU accession suspended. US/EU maintaining targeted sanctions.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR under Soviet Union. Soviet political system — no civil liberties in Western sense. April 1989 massacre imminent. FH does not cover Soviet republics as separate units.

1989.assessment: Soviet Georgia. April 9, 1989 massacre (Soviet troops kill 20 demonstrators). Independence movement growing. No civil liberties — but glasnost opening spaces. FH USSR CL=7 (worst).

1990.assessment: Soviet collapse approaching. Gamsakhurdia wins multiparty elections. Civil liberties improving from Soviet baseline but ethnic conflict reducing liberties for minorities. Georgia increasingly on FH radar as sovereign.

1991.assessment: Independence declared April 1991. Gamsakhurdia presidency increasingly authoritarian. Opposition press closures. Journalists arrested. December 1991 coup — civil war in Tbilisi streets. Freedom House: Not Free. CL=5 → inverted score = 2. Multiple wars (South Ossetia) create emergency conditions suppressing liberties.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1992

1992.assessment: Civil war (Abkhazia starts August 1992). Zviadist rebellion in western Georgia. Paramilitaries (Mkhedrioni) operating with impunity. War conditions severely restrict liberties. Ethnic cleansing in Abkhazia — Georgian population's liberties violated. FH: Not Free. CL=5 → inverted=2.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1993

1993.assessment: Peak crisis year. Abkhazia falls (September 1993). 250,000 displaced. Zviadist rebellion. Paramilitaries. Criminal violence widespread. State unable to protect basic rights. FH: Not Free. CL=5 → inverted=2.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1994

1994.assessment: Post-war stabilization. Ceasefires in place. Still Not Free. Mkhedrioni paramilitaries active (disarmed 1995). Press under government pressure. Political prisoners. Courts not independent. FH: Not Free. CL=5 → inverted=2.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1995

1995.assessment: New constitution. Mkhedrioni disarmed (Ioseliani arrested). Shevardnadze consolidating. Still Not Free. August 1995 assassination attempt. Press: state TV dominates. Opposition press exists but harassed. Courts: not independent. FH 1995: CL=5 → inverted=2.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1996

1996.assessment: Shevardnadze government. Formal stability but civil liberties limited. Independent media exists (Rustavi 2 TV founded 1994) but faces pressure. Police abuse widespread. Courts corruption. FH: Not Free. CL=5 → inverted=2.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1997

1997.assessment: Modest improvement. Rustavi 2 becomes major independent broadcaster. Some civil society development. Economic stabilization enabling civil society. FH upgrades to Partly Free: CL=4 → inverted=3. Still significant problems: Pankisi lawless, police abuse, court corruption.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1998

1998.assessment: Partly Free. Rustavi 2 critical of government. Civil society active. Gali conflict affects liberties in conflict zone. Police torture documented. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 1999

1999.assessment: Partly Free maintained. Pankisi chaos reduces rule of law in northwest. Opposition media (Rustavi 2) critical. Shevardnadze era declining governance. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2000

2000.assessment: Shevardnadze election (April 2000) widely criticized as fraudulent. Corruption increasing. Independent media (Rustavi 2) remains critical. Civil liberties declining from late 1990s modest gains. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2001

2001.assessment: October-November 2001: Imedi TV crisis. Shevardnadze supporters attack Georgian Public Broadcasting regulator. Rustavi 2 raid attempted. Mass protests defend media freedom. Shevardnadze cabinet resigns. Civil society resistance to media crackdown is significant. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2002

2002.assessment: Shevardnadze government losing legitimacy rapidly. Rustavi 2 maintaining independence. Civil society active — Liberty Institute, Georgian Young Lawyers' Association. Police abuse. Corruption endemic. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2003

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution (November 2003). Saakashvili, Burjanadze, Zhvania. Revolution peaceful. Free media (Rustavi 2) crucial in mobilizing protest. Civil society active. FH 2004 report: CL=4 → inverted=3 (revolution year assessed). Improvement expected.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004

2004.assessment: Post-revolution improvement. Saakashvili wins landslide (January 2004). Ajara reintegration peaceful. Anti-corruption drive (traffic police reformed). Media relatively free. Civil society active. FH upgrades: CL=3 → inverted=4. However: Saakashvili government shows authoritarian tendencies — rapid legislation, executive dominance. Still Partly Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005

2005.assessment: Saakashvili government consolidating. Reforms continue (tax reform). Media: Rustavi 2 sold to government-friendly owners (Kibar Khalvashi). Opposition TV reduced. Civil society: active but NGOs critical of government style. FH: CL=3 → inverted=4. Partly Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006

2006.assessment: Media: Imedi TV (independent) remains critical of government. Georgian state becoming stronger — police reformed, anti-corruption high. But: overcrowding in prisons (mass arrests of criminals). Civil society active. Russia spy crisis. FH: CL=3 → inverted=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007

2007.assessment: November 2007: State of emergency. Riot police disperse mass protests. Imedi TV raided and shut down. Saakashvili faces 'Patarkatsishvili crisis' (opposition businessman). Emergency decree. FH downgrades: CL=4 → inverted=3. Partly Free (downgraded). Saakashvili's authoritarian tendencies fully apparent.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008
Human Rights Watch, Crossing the Line, 2007

2008.assessment: War year (August 2008). Civil liberties in Georgian-controlled territory: normal operations maintained. War emergency but no extended state of emergency in rear areas. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3. Saakashvili wins snap presidential election (January 2008) with 53% — credible enough. Partly Free maintained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009

2009.assessment: Post-war. Opposition protests (April-November 2009). Police use force against protesters. Prison conditions terrible — systematic abuse documented. Media: Imedi TV returned (pro-government). State TV dominant. Independent media limited. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010
Human Rights Watch, Georgia: Harsh Punishment, 2010

2010.assessment: Constitutional changes strengthen presidential power. Prison population rapidly growing (mass arrests of criminals — zero tolerance policy). Prison abuse systematic. Media: limited independent broadcasting. Civil society active. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011

2011.assessment: May 2011: Opposition protests dispersed by riot police. Prison scandal brewing. Media: few independent outlets. Georgian Dream opposition forming (Bidzina Ivanishvili). FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012

2012.assessment: September 2012: Prison abuse video scandal — mass protests; Saakashvili government in crisis. Georgian Dream wins (October 2012) — first democratic transfer of power. New government releases political prisoners, begins prison reform. FH upgrades: CL=3 → inverted=4. Partly Free (improved).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013

2013.assessment: Giorgi Margvelashvili elected president (October 2013) — new constitutional system (parliamentary). Media: more pluralism, Rustavi 2 ownership contested. Penal reform. Civil liberties improving. FH: CL=3 → inverted=4. Some concerns: Georgian Dream using state resources against UNM.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014

2014.assessment: EU Association Agreement signed (June 2014). EU visa-free process underway. Civil liberties: CL=3 (FH) — Partly Free, stable. Some concerns: selective prosecution of UNM officials. Religious discrimination (Jehovah's Witnesses incidents). FH: CL=3 → inverted=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015

2015.assessment: Rustavi 2 ownership dispute — court orders ownership transfer to government-friendly entities; European Court of Human Rights orders interim suspension. Seen as media freedom attack. Civil liberties: stable at CL=3. FH: inverted=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016

2016.assessment: Georgian Dream wins parliamentary supermajority (October 2016). With 2/3 majority can change constitution. Rustavi 2 battle continues. Civil liberties stable. FH: CL=3 → inverted=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017

2017.assessment: EU visa-free travel (March 2017) — signal of democratic progress. Constitutional reform changes presidential election to indirect. Media: Rustavi 2 legal battle; commercial pressure on independent media. Civil society active. FH: CL=3 → inverted=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018

2018.assessment: Presidential election (October-November 2018): Salome Zourabichvili elected (Georgian Dream-backed) in close second round. Opposition claims irregularities. Civil liberties stable. Media: limited independent TV. Drug law reform (Constitutional Court rules criminalization unconstitutional). FH: CL=3 → inverted=4.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019

2019.assessment: June 2019: Gavrilov Night — riot police use rubber bullets against protesters. ~240 injured. FH downgrades: CL=4 → inverted=3. Also: Rustavi 2 ownership transferred to government-friendly Kibar Khalvashi (Supreme Court, 2019). Media freedom declining. Partly Free (downgraded).

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020

2020.assessment: COVID pandemic — emergency restrictions on civil liberties (gatherings, movement). Disputed elections (October 2020) — opposition boycotts parliament. Police violence against protesters post-election. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021

2021.assessment: Saakashvili arrested (October 2021). Prison hunger strike — treatment concerns. Ivanishvili officially retired but still influential. Media: Mtavari Arkhi (independent) raided. July 2021: Tbilisi Pride — far-right groups attack with police complicity; journalist Lekso Lashkarava killed. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022

2022.assessment: Russia-Ukraine war. Georgia applies for EU membership (March 2022). EU denies candidate status (June 2022 — 'potential candidate'). Civil liberties: CL=4 continuing decline. Government rhetoric increasingly nationalist/conservative. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023

2023.assessment: March 2023: First Foreign Agents Law attempt — mass protests ('I am the people'); withdrawn. EU grants candidate status (December 2023) conditionally. Media: government-aligned TV dominant; independent media harassed. FH: CL=4 → inverted=3. Partly Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024

2024.assessment: Severe downgrade. May 2024: Foreign Agents Law passes — mass protests, police rubber bullets. October 2024: disputed elections (Georgian Dream claims 54%). EU observes irregularities. November 2024: Georgian Dream suspends EU accession — triggers massive 'Shame' protests. December 2024: violent crackdown (water cannons, rubber bullets, arrests). US Magnitsky sanctions on Ivanishvili and officials. FH 2025 report: CL=5 → inverted=2. Trajectory from Partly Free toward Not Free.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025

2025.assessment: Protests continue into 2025. Georgian Dream entrenching power. EU accession suspended. US sanctions. Civil liberties continue to decline. Estimated CL=5 (FH) → inverted=2. Risk of further downgrade to Not Free if trajectory continues.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR. Communist Party of Georgia elite under Moscow control. Party First Secretary Eduard Shevardnadze had left for Moscow (1985). New First Secretary Jumber Patiashvili. Party elite nominally cohesive under Soviet structure, but nationalism beginning to fracture loyalty.

1989.assessment: April 9, 1989 massacre — Soviet troops kill Georgian demonstrators. Patiashvili blamed (conflicting accounts). Independence movement leadership: Zviad Gamsakhurdia, Merab Kostava (different wings). Communist elite fracturing. Georgian intelligentsia increasingly nationalist. Elite split: Soviet party loyalists vs. independence movement.

1990.assessment: Multiparty elections (October 1990): Round Table coalition (Gamsakhurdia) wins. Kostava dies in car accident (October 1989) — Gamsakhurdia sole independence movement leader. Communist party loses but many figures remain. Elite: divided between Gamsakhurdia nationalists, Soviet-era communists (Shevardnadze in Moscow), and other nationalist factions.

1991.assessment: Critical fracture: December 1991 — National Guard commanders Tengiz Kitovani and Jaba Ioseliani (Mkhedrioni) turn against Gamsakhurdia. Military/paramilitary elite abandons president. Gamsakhurdia's own security forces fracture. Elite fracture enables coup. Gamsakhurdia flees to Chechnya. Elite: deeply split between Gamsakhurdia loyalists ('Zviadists') and coup coalition (Kitovani, Ioseliani, Sigua).

sources.citation: Jonathan Wheatley, Georgia from National Awakening to Rose Revolution, 2005

1992.assessment: Shevardnadze returns (March 1992) — invited by Kitovani and Ioseliani. Becomes head of State Council. Elite: coalition of warlords (Kitovani controls National Guard; Ioseliani controls Mkhedrioni), Shevardnadze providing legitimacy. Deeply unstable coalition — each faction pursuing own interests. Abkhazia war shows disorganized command. Zviadist faction in western Georgia continues resistance.

sources.citation: Suny, The Making of the Georgian Nation, 1994

1993.assessment: Peak fragmentation. Abkhazia war: military commanders (Kitovani) fail catastrophically. September 1993: Abkhazia falls and Zviadists simultaneously attack — Shevardnadze nearly overthrown. Shevardnadze surrounded in Sukhumi, evacuated by helicopter. Russia provides military support in exchange for CIS entry. Gamsakhurdia returns, declares government, dies December 1993. Elite: total fragmentation — multiple competing armed factions.

1994.assessment: Shevardnadze begins consolidation. Zviadists defeated. Ceasefires signed. Shevardnadze draws on Soviet-era networks to build new Georgian state elite. Communist-era bureaucrats, new technocrats, former Komsomol. Kitovani and Ioseliani still powerful but being co-opted. Elite: partially consolidating around Shevardnadze but warlord factions retain real power.

1995.assessment: Key year: Mkhedrioni disarmed, Jaba Ioseliani arrested (November 1995). Kitovani arrested (1995). Warlord factions eliminated as independent power centers. Shevardnadze August 1995 assassination attempt (Giorgadze) — survived. Giorgadze flees to Russia. New Georgian elite: Citizens Union of Georgia (CUG) — Shevardnadze's party. Includes Zhvania (parliament chair), Saakashvili (joined later). Relative elite cohesion forming around Shevardnadze.

sources.citation: Wheatley, Georgia from National Awakening to Rose Revolution, 2005

1996.assessment: Citizens Union of Georgia (CUG) dominates. Relative elite cohesion under Shevardnadze. But: corruption networks proliferating — different elite factions control different state resources and patronage flows. Rustavi 2 TV (1994) provides space for different elite voices. Underlying tensions between reformists (Saakashvili, Zhvania) and Shevardnadze old guard.

1997.assessment: CUG elite stable. Saakashvili (Justice Minister 1999 forthcoming) and Zhvania (Parliament Speaker) rising. Economic reformists within CUG clash with older patronage networks. Shevardnadze managing intra-elite tensions through patronage. Stable but increasingly brittle cohesion.

1998.assessment: Intra-CUG tensions intensify. Saakashvili increasingly critical of corruption. Gali military failure (May 1998) embarrasses government. Shevardnadze's authority declining. Elite factions jockeying for post-Shevardnadze positioning. Stable elite cohesion deteriorating.

1999.assessment: Saakashvili becomes Justice Minister (1999). Attempts anti-corruption work — blocked by old guard. Parliamentary elections (October 1999): CUG wins with fraud. Zhvania: Parliament Speaker. Shevardnadze increasingly dependent on corrupt networks. Elite split deepening: reformist wing (Saakashvili, Zhvania, Burjanadze) vs. Shevardnadze corrupt patronage network.

2000.assessment: Fraudulent presidential election (April 2000). Saakashvili increasingly outspoken against corruption within government. His anti-corruption efforts blocked. Zhvania distances from Shevardnadze. Elite fracture between reformists who want to change the system and Shevardnadze network who profits from it.

2001.assessment: DECISIVE FRACTURE: Saakashvili resigns from Justice Ministry and CUG (September 2001) — citing endemic corruption. Zhvania resigns as Parliament Speaker. Both form opposition parties (Saakashvili: United National Movement; Zhvania: United Democrats). Burjanadze (Parliament Speaker) increasingly critical. TV crisis (October-November 2001) — Shevardnadze cabinet resigns. Formal split of Georgian political elite.

sources.citation: Wheatley, Georgia from National Awakening to Rose Revolution, 2005

2002.assessment: Two-bloc elite structure: Shevardnadze old guard (CUG, For New Georgia) vs. reformist opposition (UNM-Saakashvili, Burjanadze Democrats, United Democrats-Zhvania). Saakashvili wins Tbilisi mayor (unconfirmed). Kmara student movement. Soros Foundation-funded civil society pushing change. Shevardnadze increasingly isolated — can't deliver services, legitimacy gone.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution (November 2003). November 2: parliamentary elections — massive fraud documented. November 22-23: Protesters (led by Saakashvili) storm parliament. CRITICAL MOMENT: Interior Ministry and security forces refuse to fire on protesters. Shevardnadze resigns (November 23). Elite fracture decisive — security apparatus sides with opposition. New triumvirate: Saakashvili (UNM), Zhvania (United Democrats), Burjanadze (parliament). Ivanishvili: private businessman not yet in politics.

sources.citation: Cory Welt, How Strategic is Georgia's Democratic Revolution?, 2004

2004.assessment: Post-revolution elite: Saakashvili (President), Zhvania (PM), Burjanadze (Parliament Speaker). Cohesive on anti-corruption agenda but personality tensions. Ajara success (May 2004) strengthens Saakashvili. New anti-corruption elites (young technocrats — 'Misha's kids'). Former Shevardnadze officials either arrested/fled or co-opted. Early-stage elite cohesion — high on agenda, personal tensions developing.

2005.assessment: February 3, 2005: Prime Minister Zurab Zhvania dies (accidental gas poisoning — officially; conspiracy theories persist). Key coalition partner gone. Saakashvili becomes dominant. Burjanadze remains Parliament Speaker but relations with Saakashvili increasingly strained. Elite: Saakashvili dominant; UNM increasingly presidential court rather than coalition. Second-tier leaders: Vano Merabishvili (Interior), Giorgi Baramidze, Giga Bokeria.

2006.assessment: Saakashvili dominant. Burjanadze increasingly marginalized within UNM-aligned institutions. Russia spy crisis creates external pressure that temporarily unifies elite. Kodori operation (Saakashvili unilateral decision). Inner circle: Merabishvili (feared Interior Minister), Bokeria, Baramidze. Saakashvili making increasingly unilateral decisions — inner circle becoming yes-men. Elite: nominally cohesive under Saakashvili dominance.

2007.assessment: November 2007: Mass protests. Arkady ('Badri') Patarkatsishvili (billionaire oligarch) emerges as opposition backer. State of emergency. Burjanadze increasingly at odds with Saakashvili — she eventually leaves UNM in 2008. Snap presidential election (January 2008). Elite: cracking — Burjanadze exit forthcoming; Patarkatsishvili as challenger dies (February 2008); Saakashvili inner circle narrowing.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Crossing the Line, 2007

2008.assessment: February 2008: Patarkatsishvili dies of heart attack (conspiracy theories). Presidential snap election (January 2008): Saakashvili 53% — credible. Burjanadze leaves UNM (2008) — forms Democratic Movement. August 2008 war: temporary elite unity under external threat. Post-war: Saakashvili government under pressure. Merabishvili remains key. Inner circle increasingly closed.

2009.assessment: Mass protests (April-November 2009). Burjanadze joins street protests — former parliament speaker now opposition. Former UNM members defecting. Elite increasingly split: Saakashvili inner circle (Merabishvili, Bokeria) vs. growing opposition (Burjanadze, former UNM, old opposition). Ivanishvili still in background as businessman.

2010.assessment: UNM dominates parliament but increasingly autocratic. Opposition: fragmented — Christian Democrats, National Democrats, Burjanadze's movement, Alasania's Free Democrats. Ivanishvili wealth building political connections but not yet declared. Elite cohesion: UNM formally cohesive, opposition fragmented, Ivanishvili as potential kingmaker in background.

2011.assessment: October 2011: Bidzina Ivanishvili announces political entry — billionaire (wealth ~6.4B = 50% of GDP) forms Georgian Dream coalition. UNM attempts to strip his Georgian citizenship (he had French/Russian citizenship). This major elite transformation — new anti-Saakashvili pole emerges with financial resources to match UNM. Elite: UNM (Saakashvili/Merabishvili/Bokeria) vs. Georgian Dream forming (Ivanishvili + former UNM + opposition fragments).

2012.assessment: Prison scandal (September 2012) — torture videos: Saakashvili government collapses in public opinion. October 1, 2012: Georgian Dream wins elections. KEY MOMENT: Saakashvili concedes — first democratic transfer. Georgian Dream coalition: Ivanishvili + Free Democrats (Alasania) + Republicans + others. Georgian Dream internally heterogeneous. Saakashvili remains president (until 2013). Cohabitation. Elite: GD wins but internally diverse — Ivanishvili dominant.

2013.assessment: Ivanishvili resigns as PM (October 2013) after Margvelashvili elected president. Says he will leave politics — but remains behind the scenes as GD oligarchic backer. Irakli Garibashvili becomes PM. GD coalition: tensions between Ivanishvili loyalists, Alasania's Free Democrats, Republicans. Saakashvili leaves Georgia. Elite: GD formally cohesive but Ivanishvili shadow control beginning.

2014.assessment: November 2014: GD coalition fractures. Defense Minister Irakli Alasania (Free Democrats) fired after corruption charges against subordinates — Alasania calls it political persecution. Free Democrats leave coalition. GD loses coalition character; becomes Ivanishvili vehicle. Ukraine crisis: GD government cautious on Russia — contrast with Saakashvili-era Western alignment. EU AA signed June 2014.

sources.citation: ICG, Georgia: Avoiding the Trap of Cohabitation, 2014

2015.assessment: GD increasingly monolithic under Ivanishvili (officially 'retired'). Garibashvili PM. Rustavi 2 legal battle begins. UNM: Saakashvili abroad (Ukrainian MP from 2015), party fragmented in his absence. Republicans and other small parties struggling. Elite: GD dominant under Ivanishvili shadow; opposition fragmented.

2016.assessment: October 2016: GD wins supermajority (76 seats). Giorgi Kvirikashvili PM (Garibashvili stepped down). Ivanishvili 'returns' to lead GD (officially). Elite: GD highly cohesive under Ivanishvili. Opposition: UNM fragmented; European Georgia splits from UNM (2017). Alliance of Patriots (pro-Russian) small. No credible opposition. Total GD elite dominance.

2017.assessment: Constitutional reform (2017): presidential election becomes indirect from 2024. EU visa-free (March 2017). GD elite cohesive. European Georgia (Bokeria, Baramidze — former Saakashvili inner circle) now opposition. Ivanishvili controls GD without formal position (mostly). PM Kvirikashvili relatively independent — tensions developing.

2018.assessment: June 2018: Kvirikashvili resigns (tensions with Ivanishvili). Mamuka Bakhtadze becomes PM. October-November 2018: Salome Zourabichvili elected president (GD-backed).
