Ivanishvili publicly returns as GD chair. Elite: GD cohesive under Ivanishvili control; PM is now clearly Ivanishvili subordinate. Margvelashvili (outgoing president) increasingly critical of GD.

2019.assessment: June 2019: Gavrilov Night — GD internal crisis. Bakhtadze resigns (September 2019). Giorgi Gakharia (former Interior Minister, police crackdown associated) becomes PM. Rustavi 2 ownership transferred. Opposition Saakashvili's UNM calling for boycott. Elite: GD highly cohesive under Ivanishvili. Security service (GIS) under GD control. No credible elite challenge.

2020.assessment: October 2020 elections: disputed. GD wins with ~48%. Opposition boycotts parliament. COVID emergency powers. Ivanishvili 'retires' again (January 2021). GD elite: cohesive but Gakharia increasingly showing independence (will resign over Saakashvili arrest 2021). Elite: GD cohesive formally; Ivanishvili always shadow leader.

2021.assessment: February 2021: Gakharia resigns as PM over decision to arrest opposition leader Nika Melia — breaks with GD. Forms own party (For Georgia). First GD PM to resign in protest. Irakli Garibashvili returns as PM. Tbilisi Pride violence (July) — police complicity, journalist killed. Saakashvili arrested October 2021. Elite: GD loses Gakharia; but Ivanishvili shadow rule intact. First crack in GD since 2014 (Alasania).

2022.assessment: Russia-Ukraine war (February 2022). GD elite debates response — Garibashvili refuses to join sanctions against Russia. Internal tensions between pro-European and Russia-accommodating factions. EU denies candidate status (June 2022). Georgia Dream elites publicly pro-EU but policy Russia-neutral. Ivanishvili increasingly anti-Western in rhetoric. Elite: GD cohesive despite tensions.

2023.assessment: March 2023: Foreign Agents Law attempt — withdrawn under protest. Ivanishvili increasingly visible, anti-NGO rhetoric. 'Global War Party' speech (May 2024 foreshadowed). EU grants conditional candidate status (December 2023). GD elite: cohesive under Ivanishvili but clear ideological shift — from nominally pro-EU to increasingly anti-Western. For Georgia (Gakharia) and European Georgia remain small opposition.

2024.assessment: May 2024: Foreign Agents Law passed despite mass protests. October 2024: Disputed elections — GD claims 54%, observers note irregularities. November 2024: GD suspends EU accession to 2028 — triggers largest protests since Rose Revolution ('Shame'). US Magnitsky sanctions on Ivanishvili and GD officials. EU suspends accession. GD elite: highly cohesive in supporting Ivanishvili's turn. KEY FRACTURE: President Zourabichvili refuses to accept GD election results — legitimacy crisis. Isolated defectors from GD (Kobakhidze: PM; but no major splits). International isolation.

sources.citation: US Treasury, Magnitsky sanctions on Georgian officials, 2024
EU Council, suspension of Georgia accession, 2024

2025.assessment: Protests continuing. Georgian Dream elite maintains cohesion under Ivanishvili. Zourabichvili as president-without-state — moral authority but no institutional power. For Georgia, European Georgia: small but vocal opposition. No major GD defections visible as of early 2025. International isolation deepening. Elite cohesion: formal GD cohesion; but legitimacy increasingly questioned domestically and internationally.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR. Soviet institutions. Communist Party dominates all institutional life. No separation of powers, no independent judiciary, no competitive elections. Soviet institutional framework.

1989.assessment: Soviet Georgia. Glasnost opening some political space. Georgian independence movement growing. April 9, 1989 massacre galvanizes nationalism. Soviet institutional framework breaking down but replacement not established.

1990.assessment: First multiparty elections (October 1990): Round Table coalition wins. Gamsakhurdia becomes chairman of Supreme Council. Soviet institutional structures remain but Georgian alternative emerging. No stable institutional framework yet.

1991.assessment: Independence declared April 1991. Gamsakhurdia presidency increasingly authoritarian — opposition press closed, rivals arrested. December 1991: coup. Institutions collapsed — paramilitaries (Mkhedrioni, National Guard) replace state. Courts, police, civil service dysfunctional. V-Dem LDI estimated ~15/100. Worst institutional period.

1992.assessment: State Council (Shevardnadze, Kitovani, Ioseliani) replaces coup. No constitutional legitimacy. Abkhazia war begins August 1992. Multiple warlords. Mkhedrioni operate as parallel power structure. Courts dysfunctional. Elections postponed. Institutional functioning near-zero. V-Dem LDI estimated ~12/100.

1993.assessment: Peak crisis. September 1993: Abkhazia falls + Zviadist rebellion simultaneously. Shevardnadze nearly overthrown. Georgia joins CIS under Russian duress. Multiple armed factions. No functioning judicial, police, or civil service in most regions. V-Dem LDI ~12/100.

1994.assessment: Stabilization begins. Ceasefires in Abkhazia and South Ossetia. Shevardnadze consolidates as legitimate leader. Mkhedrioni still operating. Currency reform (lari preparation). Institutions beginning to re-function but still weak. Elections scheduled. V-Dem LDI estimated ~18/100.

1995.assessment: New constitution (August 1995). Mkhedrioni disarmed. Lari currency introduced. Parliamentary and presidential elections (November 1995) — credible enough (Shevardnadze 72%). Institutions beginning to function. Courts still corrupt/dependent. Police still predatory. V-Dem LDI estimated ~22/100.

1996.assessment: Continuing modest institutional development. Tax service reform begins. Some civil service restructuring. Courts remain dependent. Police corrupt. BTC pipeline negotiations require some institutional stability for international investors. V-Dem LDI ~24/100.

1997.assessment: Modest institutional functioning. Parliament operating. Independent media (Rustavi 2) emerging. Civil society developing. Courts remain corrupt/dependent on executive. Police extortion widespread. V-Dem LDI ~25/100.

1998.assessment: Gali conflict (May 1998) sets back stability slightly. Government demonstrates inability to control paramilitaries. Otherwise institutional functioning stable at modest level. V-Dem LDI ~24/100.

1999.assessment: Institutions stable at low level. Shevardnadze government aging and increasingly corrupt. Elections increasingly managed. Courts remain dependent. Police predatory. V-Dem LDI ~24/100.

2000.assessment: Presidential election (April 2000) — widely criticized as fraudulent (Shevardnadze 79%). Institutional functioning declining. Corruption worsening. Power cuts, unpaid salaries. State increasingly unable to provide basic services. V-Dem LDI ~22/100.

2001.assessment: October-November 2001: Imedi TV/Rustavi 2 crisis — government attempts media control. Shevardnadze cabinet resigns. Burjanadze, Saakashvili, Zhvania emerge as opposition. Institutional functioning declining further. Power blackouts, unpaid teachers. Government legitimacy collapsing. V-Dem LDI ~21/100.

2002.assessment: Pre-revolutionary period. Shevardnadze government effectively collapsed in terms of legitimacy and service delivery. Tbilisi power cuts widespread. Opposition consolidates. Civil society active (Kmara student movement beginning). Institutions hollow — formal structure exists but functions poorly. V-Dem LDI ~20/100.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution (November 2003). Peaceful transfer of power — institutions survive. Parliament re-opens with Burjanadze as speaker. Saakashvili, Zhvania, Burjanadze form interim government. Snap elections called. Institutional functioning: weak but transitioning. V-Dem LDI ~22/100.

2004.assessment: Post-revolution surge. Saakashvili wins 96% (snap election January 2004 — credibility questioned). Rapid institutional reforms: police reform (traffic police abolished and replaced), anti-corruption sweep, tax administration reformed. Revenue collection increases dramatically. Anti-corruption prosecutions. V-Dem LDI estimated ~35/100. Sharp improvement.

sources.citation: World Bank, Georgia: Poverty and Inequality, 2009

2005.assessment: Institutional reforms deepening. Tax reform completing. Customs reform. Ease of doing business improvements (Georgian Doing Business rankings rise sharply). Flat tax (20% income tax). But: parliament weak relative to strong presidency. Courts remain executive-influenced. Civil society critical of executive dominance. V-Dem LDI ~38/100.

2006.assessment: Institutional functioning maintained. Kodori operation (successful but shows executive unilateralism). Russia spy crisis — handled through formal diplomatic channels. Anti-corruption: prosecutions continue. Prison population growing (zero tolerance). Judiciary: executive pressure. V-Dem LDI ~38/100.

2007.assessment: November 2007: State of emergency. Imedi TV raided. Protests dispersed. Saakashvili calls snap presidential election (January 2008). Institutional regression — executive overreach. V-Dem LDI declines to ~33/100.

2008.assessment: War year (August). Institutions survive war — no collapse. Snap presidential election (January 2008) credible-ish. Post-war: institutions continue functioning in Georgian-controlled territory. Institutional functioning slightly lower due to war emergency. V-Dem LDI ~32/100.

2009.assessment: Post-war political crisis. Mass opposition protests (April-November 2009). Saakashvili government under pressure. Institutions functioning but under stress. Prison abuse rising. Courts: executive dominated. Police: reformed but increasingly oppressive. V-Dem LDI ~30/100.

2010.assessment: Constitutional changes strengthen presidency further. Prison population: Georgia has one of highest incarceration rates globally (per capita). Prison conditions: systematic abuse. Judiciary: weakly independent. Parliament: weakly effective. V-Dem LDI ~30/100.

2011.assessment: Institutional functioning declining under Saakashvili later years. Opposition repressed. Prison abuse systematic. Ivanishvili announces political entry (October 2011) — government attempts to strip his citizenship. V-Dem LDI ~29/100.

2012.assessment: Georgian Dream wins (October 2012) — first peaceful democratic transfer. Saakashvili accepts defeat — key institutional moment. New government begins prison reform, releases political prisoners. Institutional functioning improves. V-Dem LDI ~33/100.

2013.assessment: New constitutional system (parliamentary republic) takes effect. Margvelashvili elected president — independent from Georgian Dream. Ivanishvili resigns PM after 1 year. Institutions: improving judiciary independence, press freedom. Prison reform continuing. V-Dem LDI ~36/100.

2014.assessment: EU Association Agreement signed — institutional reform roadmap. Judiciary: continued reform efforts. Georgian Dream government consolidating but more institutionally respectful than Saakashvili's later years. Concerns: selective prosecution of UNM officials. V-Dem LDI ~37/100.

2015.assessment: Rustavi 2 ownership dispute begins — court system used for political ends. Concerns about judiciary independence. Otherwise: institutions functioning. Civil society active. EU requirements driving reform compliance. V-Dem LDI ~37/100.

2016.assessment: Georgian Dream supermajority (76/150 seats). Constitutional changes possible without opposition. Concerns about concentration of power. Institutions formally functioning but GD dominance reduces checks. V-Dem LDI ~36/100.

2017.assessment: Constitutional reform reduces presidential powers (2020 effect). EU visa-free begins. Rustavi 2 dispute continues. Georgian Dream reshapes judiciary through appointments. V-Dem LDI ~35/100.

2018.assessment: Presidential election: Zourabichvili wins narrowly in second round. Institutional functioning maintained — elections credible enough. Concerns: Georgian Dream control of courts, state broadcaster. V-Dem LDI ~34/100.

2019.assessment: Gavrilov Night — police brutality against protesters. Rustavi 2 ownership transferred. Institutional regression. V-Dem LDI ~32/100.

2020.assessment: Disputed parliamentary elections (October 2020). Opposition boycotts parliament. COVID emergency used to restrict opposition activities. Institutional functioning declining. V-Dem LDI ~30/100.

2021.assessment: Saakashvili arrested. Tbilisi Pride violence (police complicity). Journalist killed. Political prisoner concerns. Ivanishvili 'retiring' but clearly controlling GD. Courts used politically. V-Dem LDI ~28/100.

2022.assessment: EU candidate status denied (only 'potential candidate'). Institutional functioning declining further. Georgian Dream rhetoric increasingly nationalist/anti-Western. Courts: GD loyalists in key positions. V-Dem LDI ~28/100.

2023.assessment: Foreign Agents Law attempt (March 2023) withdrawn under protest. EU grants conditional candidate status (December 2023). Georgian Dream increasingly aligning rhetoric with Hungary's Orbán model. Institutional functioning declining — parliament rubber-stamp, courts GD-aligned, civil society under pressure. V-Dem LDI ~26/100.

2024.assessment: Severe institutional regression. May 2024: Foreign Agents Law passed — against EU recommendations. October 2024: disputed elections (OSCE/ODIHR notes irregularities). November 2024: Georgian Dream suspends EU accession process — parliament vote. Mass protests. US sanctions on Ivanishvili. EU suspends accession. Institutional functioning at lowest since early 2000s. V-Dem LDI estimated ~22/100.

2025.assessment: Protests continuing. Georgian Dream entrenching. EU accession suspended. US sanctions. Institutions at lowest point since Shevardnadze late era. Trajectory: continued decline toward competitive authoritarian system. V-Dem LDI estimated ~21/100.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR under Soviet Union. April 1989: Soviet troops kill 20 Georgian demonstrators in Tbilisi (Bloody Sunday, April 9). Early ethnic tensions in Abkhazia and South Ossetia but full-scale conflict not yet begun.

1989.assessment: April 9, 1989: Soviet troops disperse Georgian independence demonstration with sharpened shovels and toxic gas — 20 killed, hundreds injured. This 'Bloody Sunday' galvanizes Georgian independence movement. July 1989: Inter-ethnic clashes in Abkhazia (Sukhumi) — ~16 killed. South Ossetia: early clashes. ~50 estimated conflict-related deaths.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Conflict in Georgia, 1990

1990.assessment: Escalating ethnic violence in South Ossetia. Georgian irregular forces and South Ossetian militia clash. Georgian government abolishes South Ossetian autonomy (December 1990). Inter-ethnic killings, displacement beginning. Abkhazia: tensions but not yet open war. ~100 estimated deaths.

1991.assessment: South Ossetia war intensifies (January-June 1991). Georgian National Guard and Mkhedrioni paramilitaries vs. South Ossetian militia. Heavy fighting in and around Tskhinvali. ~500 deaths estimated 1991. December 1991: coup against Gamsakhurdia — National Guard commanders Kitovani and Ioseliani lead Military Council assault on Tbilisi. Street fighting in Tbilisi. Gamsakhurdia flees January 1992.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Georgia: Crisis in the Mkhedrioni, 1994

1992.assessment: June 1992: South Ossetia ceasefire (Russian-brokered). South Ossetia war total deaths ~1,000-2,000 (1990-1992 combined). August 1992: Georgia sends National Guard into Abkhazia — Abkhazia war begins. Georgian forces capture Sukhumi initially. Major atrocities. Gamsakhurdia loyalists ('Zviadists') active in western Georgia. Multi-front civil war conditions. ~1,000 deaths in 1992.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Georgia/Abkhazia: Violations of Laws of War, 1995

1993.assessment: Peak violence year. Abkhazia war: September 1993 Abkhazian forces (with Russian military involvement — Black Sea Fleet, North Caucasian volunteers) capture Sukhumi. ~10,000 deaths in the Abkhazia conflict (1992-93 combined), mass atrocities. ~250,000 Georgians/Mingrelians expelled from Abkhazia in weeks. Simultaneously: Gamsakhurdia loyalists (Zviadists) mount rebellion in western Georgia — Shevardnadze nearly overthrown. Georgia joins CIS to get Russian support. Gamsakhurdia dies December 1993 (suicide or killed).

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Georgia/Abkhazia: Violations of Laws of War, 1995
UN Security Council documents on Georgia/Abkhazia, 1993

1994.assessment: Abkhazia ceasefire (May 1994). CIS peacekeepers deployed. Sporadic violence along ceasefire lines. Gali district: Georgian guerrillas ('White Legion,' 'Forest Brothers') conduct raids into Abkhazia. Kidnappings. Zviadists defeated. Sharply reduced but not zero violence. ~100 deaths estimated.

1995.assessment: Gali district: continued guerrilla activity, kidnappings, ambushes. August 1995: assassination attempt on Shevardnadze — bomb at State Chancellery. Shevardnadze survives. Igoris Giorgadze (security chief) blamed, flees to Russia. Sporadic violence. Criminal-political nexus (Mkhedrioni). ~50 estimated deaths.

1996.assessment: Continuing low-level violence in Gali district. Mkhedrioni disarmed (Jaba Ioseliani arrested 1995). Paramilitaries integrated into formal structures or demobilized. Pankisi Gorge beginning to see Chechen refugees. Criminal violence high. Political violence declining. ~30 estimated deaths.

1997.assessment: Low-level violence continues in Gali district — occasional ambushes, kidnappings. Political violence low in core Georgia. Pankisi: Chechen refugees increasing. ~20 estimated conflict deaths.

1998.assessment: May 1998: Major flare-up — Georgian paramilitaries and Abkhazian forces clash in Gali district ('Second Georgian-Abkhaz War'). ~200 deaths, ~30,000 Georgians re-displaced from Gali. Abkhazian forces consolidate Gali. CIS peacekeepers fail to prevent. UNOMIG monitors. Violence subsides by July 1998.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, A Cycle of Violence, 1998

1999.assessment: Post-Gali district conflict low violence. Pankisi Gorge: Chechen refugees and fighters increasing (second Chechen war). Kidnappings in Pankisi — high-profile cases. Otherwise Georgia's political violence relatively low. ~30 estimated deaths.

2000.assessment: Low-level violence. Pankisi Gorge: Georgian government lacks control; kidnappings continue; alleged al-Qaeda linked fighters transit. Russian pressure on Georgia. State violence (police torture) documented by NGOs. ~20 estimated conflict deaths.

2001.assessment: October 2001: Russian military helicopter strikes inside Georgian territory near Pankisi. Georgia protests. Post-9/11: US focus on Pankisi as terrorist transit. Imedi TV crisis — Shevardnadze supporters vs. Burjanadze/Saakashvili opposition. Low conflict deaths but political tensions very high. ~25 estimated deaths.

2002.assessment: US Georgia Train and Equip Program (GTEP) trains Georgian military for counter-terrorism in Pankisi. Pankisi Gorge operation partially clears militants. Ceasefire lines quiet. Low political violence. ~15 estimated deaths.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution (November 2003) — remarkably peaceful. Protesters enter parliament; Shevardnadze resigns without bloodshed. No significant political violence during the revolution itself. Frozen conflict lines quiet. ~10 estimated conflict-related deaths (sporadic Gali/Pankisi incidents).

sources.citation: ICG, Saakashvili's Ajara Success: Repeatable Elsewhere?, 2004

2004.assessment: Ajara reintegration (May 2004): peaceful — Abashidze flees to Russia after protests; no bloodshed. South Ossetia: Summer 2004 Georgian-South Ossetian firefights — Georgian Interior Ministry special forces vs. South Ossetian militia. ~20-30 deaths in South Ossetia summer clashes. Ceasefire restored. Abkhazia quiet. ~50 estimated total deaths.

sources.citation: ICG, Georgia: Avoiding War in South Ossetia, 2004
Human Rights Watch, Up in Flames, 2009

2005.assessment: Post-2004 South Ossetia clashes: ceasefire holding. Russian base withdrawal agreements. Ajara peaceful. Low political violence. Some Pankisi incidents. ~15 estimated deaths. Georgia military modernizing with US/NATO support.

2006.assessment: Kodori Gorge operation (July 2006): Georgian special forces expel warlord Kvitsiani — relatively limited violence. Russia-Georgia spy crisis (September 2006): diplomatic/economic retaliation, not military. Ceasefire lines quiet. ~10 conflict deaths estimated.

2007.assessment: Tensions rising pre-war. November 2007: Saakashvili declares state of emergency — riot police disperse mass opposition protests; Imedi TV raided. Ceasefire line incidents increasing in South Ossetia. ~20 estimated deaths (conflict + political). State of emergency ends after 9 days.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Crossing the Line, 2007

2008.assessment: August 7-12, 2008: Russia-Georgia War. Georgia attacks South Ossetia (August 7-8). Russia responds with massive counter-invasion (August 8). Russian forces advance through Gori to within 40km of Tbilisi. Abkhazian forces expel Georgians from Kodori Gorge. Ceasefire August 12 (Sarkozy plan). Deaths: ~170 Georgian military, ~400 Russian/South Ossetian military, ~280 civilians (various estimates). Tagliavini Report (EU-commissioned): ~850 total deaths. Massive destruction in Gori, South Ossetia. ~26,000 new IDPs.

sources.citation: Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on the Conflict in Georgia (Tagliavini Report), 2009
Human Rights Watch, Up in Flames, 2009
Amnesty International, Civilians in the Line of Fire, 2008

2009.assessment: Post-war. Ceasefire lines hardened. EUMM deployed. Very few conflict deaths. Borderization begins but no major armed incidents. Some Gali district incidents. Political stability restored in Georgian-controlled territory. ~5 estimated conflict deaths.

2010.assessment: Very low conflict deaths. Borderization incidents cause deaths in rare cases (detentions, accidents). EUMM monitors ceasefire lines. No armed clashes. Political violence in Georgian-controlled territory low. ~5 estimated.

2011.assessment: Near-zero conflict deaths. EUMM continues monitoring. Political violence low in Georgian-controlled territory. Prison abuse scandal (2012 forthcoming) — signs of systemic state violence in penal system. ~5 estimated conflict deaths.

2012.assessment: September 2012: Prison abuse video scandal — guards torturing inmates; mass protests. Major political crisis. Georgian Dream wins elections. Despite political turmoil, no significant inter-party violence. Frozen conflict lines quiet. EUMM monitors. ~5 conflict deaths estimated.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Harsh Punishment: Ill-Treatment and Torture in Georgia's Prisons, 2012

2013.assessment: Near-zero conflict deaths. Borderization incidents. Georgian Dream government pursues penal reform. Political violence very low. Frozen conflict lines quiet. ~5 estimated.

2014.assessment: Ukraine Crimea annexation (February 2014) raises Georgia's anxiety. No domestic military conflict. Borderization incidents. EUMM monitoring. Political violence near-zero. ~5 estimated.

2015.assessment: Near-zero conflict deaths. Stable frozen conflict lines. Borderization incidents (South Ossetia fence movements). Political violence very low. ~5 estimated.

2016.assessment: Near-zero conflict deaths. Borderization incidents. Political violence low. ~5 estimated.

2017.assessment: EU Schengen visa-free begins. Near-zero conflict deaths. Borderization incidents. ~5 estimated.

2018.assessment: Near-zero conflict deaths. Borderization incidents. Political violence low. ~5 estimated.

2019.assessment: June 2019: Gavrilov Night — riot police disperse protesters with rubber bullets and tear gas. ~240 injured, 1-2 deaths reported. Archi Tatunashvili death in South Ossetia custody (2018, reported 2019) — borderization-related death. Low overall conflict deaths but political violence incidents. ~10 total estimated.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Georgia: Excessive Force Against Protesters, 2019

2020.assessment: COVID pandemic. Reduced protest activity. Borderization incidents. Near-zero conflict deaths. Disputed elections (October 2020) — some protests but limited violence. ~5 estimated.

2021.assessment: Saakashvili arrested on return to Georgia (October 2021). Prison hunger strike. Protests. Borderization incidents. Low overall conflict deaths. ~10 estimated.

2022.assessment: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine (February 2022). Georgia does not join war. Mass anti-war sentiment in Georgia (spontaneous pro-Ukraine rallies). Georgian government cautious — refuses sanctions on Russia. Near-zero domestic conflict deaths. ~5 estimated.

2023.assessment: March 2023: First Foreign Agents Law attempt — mass protests; government withdraws bill. Police use water cannons, tear gas. Protests continue periodically. Low conflict deaths overall. ~10 estimated (including police use of force incidents).

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Georgia: Events of 2023

2024.assessment: Escalating state violence against protesters. May 2024: Foreign Agents Law passes — major protests, police use force. October 2024: disputed parliamentary elections. November 2024: Georgian Dream announces suspension of EU accession — triggers massive 'Shame' protests. December 2024: police use rubber bullets, water cannons, tear gas against protesters. Multiple serious injuries. Security forces use excessive force. EU and US condemn police actions. ~50 estimated serious violence incidents/deaths. Political violence level highest since 2007-2008.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Georgia: Events of 2024
US State Department statement on Georgia, 2024

2025.assessment: Protests continue into 2025. Georgian Dream consolidates control. Police use of force against protesters. Western pressure through sanctions. Frozen conflicts unchanged. ~30 estimated serious violence incidents. Trajectory: escalating domestic political violence while frozen conflict lines remain stable.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR under Soviet Union. Full territorial integrity as part of USSR. South Ossetia AO and Abkhaz ASSR are autonomous units within Georgian SSR — no de facto separation yet.

1989.assessment: Soviet Georgia. Tensions begin. South Ossetia Council votes to upgrade to SSR (November 1989). Abkhazia Supreme Soviet votes to restore 1925 constitution. Georgian government rejects both. Conflict embryonic but territorial control remains Soviet/Georgian state.

sources.citation: ICG, Georgia: Avoiding War in South Ossetia, 2004

1990.assessment: Georgia declares sovereignty (November 1990). Gamsakhurdia leads Round Table coalition. South Ossetia declares independence (December 1990). Georgian government abolishes South Ossetian autonomy. Inter-ethnic violence begins in South Ossetia. Abkhazia also pushing autonomy demands.

sources.citation: Thomas de Waal, The Caucasus, Oxford 2010

1991.assessment: Georgia declares independence (April 1991). South Ossetia war intensifies — Georgian paramilitaries (Mkhedrioni) and National Guard in South Ossetia capital Tskhinvali. Abkhazia still under Georgian administration. Gamsakhurdia overthrown December 1991 / January 1992 coup. Territorial control approximately 90% (South Ossetia in active conflict ~3%).

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Georgia/South Ossetia: Crackdown in the Capital, 1991

1992.assessment: Shevardnadze returns, becomes head of State Council. June 1992: Russian-brokered ceasefire in South Ossetia — joint peacekeeping force (Georgian/Russian/Ossetian). South Ossetia de facto independent but internationally unrecognized. August 1992: Georgia sends National Guard into Abkhazia — Abkhazia war begins. By year end, Abkhazian forces with Russian support advance. ~85% territory under Tbilisi control.

1993.assessment: September 1993: Abkhazian forces (with Russian military support) capture Sukhumi, President Gamsakhurdia's supporters simultaneously attack. Georgian forces collapse. ~250,000 Georgians/Mingrelians flee Abkhazia in weeks. Shevardnadze surrounded in Sukhumi, evacuated by Russian helicopter. Georgia forced to join CIS and allow Russian military bases in exchange for Russian support against Gamsakhurdia. Territorial control reduced to ~80%.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Georgia/Abkhazia: Violations of Laws of War, 1995

1994.assessment: Ceasefire agreements stabilize 'frozen conflicts.' May 1994 Abkhazia ceasefire (Moscow Agreement) — CIS peacekeepers (Russian troops) deployed. South Ossetia Joint Peacekeeping Force (Russian-led) continues. Georgia controls ~80% of internationally recognized territory. Ajara semi-autonomous (Aslan Abashidze controls but nominally Georgian). Pankisi Gorge ungoverned.

1995.assessment: Frozen conflict lines stable. Abkhazia de facto independent with ~250K Georgian IDPs unable to return. South Ossetia under Russian/Ossetian control. New Georgian constitution adopted (1995) — claims full territorial integrity including Abkhazia and South Ossetia. Ajara — Abashidze nominally loyal. Georgia controls approximately 80% of its recognized territory.

1996.assessment: Frozen conflict lines unchanged. Abkhazia elections held (not recognized internationally). South Ossetia maintains de facto separation. Georgian government begins BTC pipeline negotiations to reduce Russian energy dependency. No territorial changes.

1997.assessment: Frozen conflicts stable. Minsk Group negotiations on Abkhazia status make no progress. South Ossetia negotiations stalled. Ajara remains nominally under Georgian sovereignty — Abashidze consolidates. ~80% control.

1998.assessment: May 1998: Second Georgian-Abkhaz war (Gali district). Georgian paramilitaries and Abkhaz forces clash. ~30,000 additional Georgians displaced from Gali. Abkhazia consolidates control. CIS sanctions on Abkhazia declared but not enforced. ~80% control maintained.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, A Cycle of Violence, 1998

1999.assessment: Istanbul OSCE Summit (November 1999): Russia agrees to close Gudauta base in Abkhazia and Vaziani base near Tbilisi. Pankisi Gorge increasingly ungoverned — Chechen refugees and fighters enter. ~80% control. Ajara still Abashidze-controlled.

2000.assessment: Frozen conflict lines unchanged. Pankisi Gorge increasingly lawless. Shevardnadze government losing control of periphery — not just Abkhazia/South Ossetia but also warlord zones. Russian pressure on Pankisi (al-Qaeda linked fighters allegedly present). ~80% internationally recognized territory under Tbilisi control.

2001.assessment: Post-9/11: US focuses on Pankisi Gorge as possible Al-Qaeda transit. Russian military strikes in Pankisi area (October 2001 helicopter). Georgia demands Russian forces leave. Georgia Train and Equip Program (GTEP) preparation begins. Frozen conflicts unchanged. Kodori Gorge (upper Abkhazia) remains Georgian-controlled — contested area.

sources.citation: ICG, Georgia: Avoiding War in South Ossetia, 2004

2002.assessment: US Georgia Train and Equip Program (GTEP) launches. US special forces train Georgian military for counter-terrorism. Russia-Georgia tensions over Pankisi. Frozen conflicts stable. BTC pipeline construction begins. Ajara: Abashidze consolidates personal militia and customs revenues.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution (November 2003). Saakashvili, Burjanadze, Zhvania replace Shevardnadze. Immediate priority: restore territorial control and state authority. Frozen conflict lines unchanged during revolution. New government announces intent to resolve Abkhazia and South Ossetia through peaceful means with Western support. Ajara crisis imminent.

sources.citation: ICG, Georgia: Avoiding War in South Ossetia, 2004

2004.assessment: May 2004: Ajara crisis resolved peacefully. Abashidze flees to Russia after mass protests; Georgian government takes control of Ajara without bloodshed — major success. ~4% territory reintegrated. South Ossetia: renewed tensions — Georgian Interior Ministry special forces enter. Summer 2004: Georgian-South Ossetian firefights. Russia signals support for South Ossetia. Overall ~84% territory under Tbilisi control post-Ajara.

sources.citation: ICG, Georgia: Avoiding War in South Ossetia, 2004
Human Rights Watch, Up in Flames, 2009

2005.assessment: May 2005: Georgia-Russia agreement on Russian military base withdrawals — Batumi (Ajara) and Akhalkalaki bases to close by 2008. Russian bases had been leverage for Russia; withdrawal reduces that leverage. Frozen conflicts stable. South Ossetia: Georgian infrastructure projects attempt to draw population. Kodori Gorge (upper Abkhazia) remains Georgian-controlled. ~84% control.

2006.assessment: July 2006: Georgian special forces enter Kodori Gorge (upper Abkhazia) — expel warlord Emzar Kvitsiani. Government of Abkhazia-in-exile relocated to Kodori. This gave Georgia de facto control of ~3% of Abkhazia territory. Russia objects. September 2006: Georgia arrests Russian military intelligence officers — major Russia-Georgia crisis. Spy crisis leads to Russian sanctions, deportations. ~84% control.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Up in Flames, 2009
ICG, Georgia and Russia: Clashing over Abkhazia, 2008

2007.assessment: Tensions escalating. Abkhazia and South Ossetia de facto governments increasingly integrated with Russia — ruble zone, Russian passports distributed. Georgia protests Russian 'passportization.' Batumi/Akhalkalaki Russian bases close per 2005 agreement (completed November 2007). Gudauta base (Abkhazia) — Russia refuses to close, citing 1999 Istanbul obligation. ~84% control pre-war.

sources.citation: ICG, Georgia and Russia: Clashing over Abkhazia, 2008

2008.assessment: August 7-12, 2008: Russia-Georgia War. Georgia attacks South Ossetia capital Tskhinvali; Russia responds with full invasion. Russian forces advance to within 40km of Tbilisi before ceasefire (Sarkozy plan August 12). Georgia loses Kodori Gorge (Abkhaz forces expel Georgians). Russian forces briefly occupy Gori, Zugdidi. August 26: Russia recognizes Abkhazia and South Ossetia as independent — first country to do so. Russian forces permanently stationed in both breakaway regions. Georgia loses ~20% territory: Abkhazia ~12% + South Ossetia ~8% (expanded 'buffer zones' around 2008). Returns to ~80% control.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Up in Flames: Humanitarian Law Violations and Civilian Victims in the Conflict over South Ossetia, 2009
ICG, Russia vs Georgia: The Fallout, 2008
Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on the Conflict in Georgia (Tagliavini Report), 2009

2009.assessment: Post-war consolidation. EUMM (EU Monitoring Mission) deployed October 2008 — monitors ceasefire lines. Russia refuses EUMM access to Abkhazia and South Ossetia. Borderization begins: Russia/breakaway forces install fences and barriers along administrative boundary lines (ABLs). ~80% Georgian control unchanged. Geneva International Discussions begin (October 2008) — framework for conflict resolution, still ongoing.

sources.citation: ICG, South Ossetia: The Burden of Recognition, 2010

2010.assessment: Borderization accelerates in South Ossetia — fences, barriers, 'creeping annexation.' Abkhazia and South Ossetia deepen integration with Russia (economic, military, administrative). Recognized by handful of states (Nicaragua, Venezuela, Nauru). Georgia enacts Law on Occupied Territories (2008) — restricts entry to breakaway regions. ~80% control stable.

2011.assessment: Borderization continues. Abkhazia and South Ossetia deepen Russian integration. No changes to control lines. Geneva Discussions continue without substantive progress. Saakashvili government maintains no-war/no-recognition policy. Georgian military rebuilding post-2008.

2012.assessment: Georgian Dream wins October 2012 elections — first peaceful democratic transfer of power. Ivanishvili government adopts more conciliatory approach toward Russia. 'Normalization' without recognition. Frozen conflicts unchanged. ~80% control.

2013.assessment: Borderization continues — South Ossetia ABL fence extensions. Some incidents of Georgian villagers waking to find fence moved overnight on their property. EUMM monitors incidents but cannot access breakaway side. ~80% control unchanged. Ivanishvili resigns as PM after one year.

2014.assessment: EU Association Agreement signed (June 2014) — amid Russia's annexation of Crimea. Georgia acutely aware of parallel with Abkhazia/South Ossetia. Russia-Ukraine war context increases Georgia's solidarity with territorial integrity norms. Frozen conflicts unchanged. Borderization continues. ~80% control.

2015.assessment: Borderization incidents continue (South Ossetia). Abkhazia signs 'Treaty on Alliance and Strategic Partnership' with Russia (November 2014) — further integration. South Ossetia formally requests unification with Russia (not granted). ~80% control. Geneva Discussions continue.

2016.assessment: South Ossetia 'Treaty on Integration and Alliance' with Russia signed (March 2015 in effect). Borderization continuing. No change to overall control percentages. EU visa liberalization for Georgia on horizon (2017). ~80% control.

2017.assessment: EU Schengen visa-free travel granted (March 2017). Frozen conflicts unchanged. Borderization incidents continuing. South Ossetia incorporates additional Georgian villages into its administrative boundary in periodic 'borderization' moves. ~80% control.

2018.assessment: Borderization continues. Georgian Dream wins parliamentary elections. Constitutional changes (2017 constitutional reform in effect). No territorial changes. EUMM continues monitoring. Geneva Discussions ongoing. ~80% control.

2019.assessment: June 2019: Russian MP Gavrilov sits in Georgian parliamentary speaker's chair during inter-parliamentary event — sparks mass protests ('Gavrilov Night'). Riot police disperse protesters. Political crisis but no territorial change. Frozen conflicts unchanged. ~80% control.

sources.citation: Reuters, Georgian protests after Russian lawmaker takes parliament speaker's seat, 2019

2020.assessment: COVID pandemic. Borderization continuing but reduced activity due to pandemic. Disputed parliamentary elections (October 2020). Frozen conflicts unchanged. ~80% control.

2021.assessment: Arrested former President Saakashvili returns to Georgia, arrested. Political crisis. Frozen conflicts unchanged. Borderization continues (South Ossetia incidents). ~80% control.

2022.assessment: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine (February 2022) sharply increases Georgia's anxiety about its own occupied territories and Russian intentions. Georgia refrains from sanctioning Russia — criticized by West. EU candidate status denied (June 2022 — Georgia gets 'potential candidate'). Russian emigres/anti-war Russians flood Georgia. Frozen conflicts unchanged. Borderization continues. ~80% control.

2023.assessment: EU grants Georgia 'candidate status' (December 2023) — conditional. Frozen conflicts unchanged. Borderization continues. South Ossetia: Russia continues deepening administrative integration. Abkhazia: internal political tensions (Abkhaz population resistant to Russian economic domination). ~80% control.

2024.assessment: Georgian Dream wins disputed elections (October 2024). Foreign Agents Law passed (May 2024) — mass protests ('Shame'). EU suspends accession process. US Magnitsky sanctions on Ivanishvili and officials. Massive protests November-December 2024. Frozen conflicts unchanged. Borderization continues. Some analysts warn Georgian Dream's Russia-accommodation could facilitate further territorial erosion. ~80% control.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Georgia: Events of 2024

2025.assessment: Protests continue into 2025. Salome Zourabichvili term ends; Georgian Dream appoints new president. Western pressure through sanctions and suspended accession. Frozen conflicts unchanged. Borderization continues. Risk of further territorial erosion if Georgia fully abandons Euro-Atlantic integration path — but no immediate change. ~80% control.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR. Soviet budget system — not publicly available; centrally planned. No budget transparency in modern sense.

1989.assessment: Soviet Georgia. No public budget. Not applicable.

1990.assessment: Soviet transitioning. No independent budget system yet. Not applicable.

1991.assessment: Independence declared. State collapse. No functioning budget system — hyperinflation, no state revenues. OBS not yet in existence. Not applicable.

1992.assessment: Civil war. Multiple armed factions. No functioning state budget in modern sense. Not applicable.

1993.assessment: Peak crisis. Hyperinflation (15,606% in 1994). Budget system non-functional. Not applicable.

1994.assessment: Stabilization beginning. Lari introduced 1995. Budget system being established. OBS first survey only in 2006. Not applicable.

1995.assessment: Lari currency introduced. New constitution. Budget system being established but no transparency mechanisms. OBS does not cover this period. Not applicable for OBS scoring.

1996.assessment: Budget system developing under Shevardnadze. No budget transparency mechanisms. OBS not yet in existence. Not applicable.

1997.assessment: Budget system functioning at basic level. IMF programs require some fiscal transparency for donors. But no public OBS coverage. Not applicable.

1998.assessment: Budget system with basic functioning. IMF PRGF program. Donor-required transparency improving slightly. No OBS. Not applicable.

1999.assessment: Budget transparency low under Shevardnadze. IMF programs requiring some disclosure. No OBS data. Not applicable.

2000.assessment: Shevardnadze era: budget transparency very limited. Power cuts, unpaid salaries — state unable to implement budget. No OBS. Not applicable.

2001.assessment: Budget transparency poor. Cabinet resigns. TV crisis. State revenues collapsing (power sector debts, unpaid pensions). No OBS. Not applicable.

2002.assessment: Pre-revolution declining governance. Budget transparency minimal. Not applicable for OBS.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution (November 2003). Reform agenda begins. Budget transparency becomes a reform priority. But OBS first survey is 2006. Not applicable for scoring.

2004.assessment: Saakashvili government: major tax reform. Revenue collection improves dramatically (200% increase). Budget transparency improving rapidly — donor-required reforms, anti-corruption agenda. OBS starts 2006. Not applicable for scoring but notable improvement.

2005.assessment: Budget transparency reforms continue. Flat tax (20%). Customs reform. Revenue systems improving. Just before OBS starts. Not applicable.

2006.assessment: First OBS survey (2006): Georgia scores 43/100. Classified as 'limited information.' Post-revolution reforms created basic budget transparency infrastructure but significant gaps. Pre-budget statement published. Budget proposal available. Year-end report weak. Audit limited. Score of 43 is modest — better than neighbors but below reform ambitions.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2006
Open Budget Index Georgia 2006 Country Report

2007.assessment: Interpolated between OBS 2006 (43) and OBS 2008 (55). Budget transparency improving with continued reform. State of emergency (November 2007) may have some negative effect but limited on budget processes. Estimated ~45.

2008.assessment: OBS 2008: Georgia 55/100 — 'some information.' Sharp improvement from 2006 (43). Budget reforms deepening: Citizens' Budget published, year-end reports improving, audit function developing. August 2008 war occurs after survey period — minimal effect on 2008 score. Georgia among improving countries in region.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2008

2009.assessment: Post-war. Budget transparency reforms continue despite economic crisis. IMF SBA (Stand-By Arrangement) requires fiscal transparency. Interpolated between 2008 (55) and 2010 (61). Estimated ~57.

2010.assessment: OBS 2010: Georgia 61/100 — 'significant information.' Further improvement. Citizens' Budget and mid-year review improving. Year-end audit function developing. Supreme Audit Institution (State Audit Office) gaining capacity. Georgia among best performers in South Caucasus/CIS region.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2010

2011.assessment: Interpolated. Budget transparency likely near peak. State Audit Office becoming functional. Estimated ~62.

2012.assessment: OBS 2012: Georgia 55/100 — notable decline from 2010 (61). Transition year (Georgian Dream wins October 2012). Budget transparency may have suffered in election period volatility. Some OBS criteria harder to meet. Still 'some information.' Concerning decline.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2012

2013.assessment: Interpolated. Georgian Dream government begins prison reforms and budget reorientation. IMF program. Transparency improving post-transition. Estimated ~58.

2014.assessment: Interpolated. EU AA requirements driving fiscal transparency improvements. Estimated ~60.

2015.assessment: OBS 2015: Georgia 62/100 — 'substantial information.' Recovery from 2012 decline. State Audit Office stronger. Program budget system being introduced. EU AA fiscal requirements driving improvements. Georgia among better performers globally (top third).

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2015

2016.assessment: Interpolated. Budget transparency continuing to improve. Estimated ~63.

2017.assessment: OBS 2017: Georgia 67/100 — peak score; 'substantial information.' Best ever OBS score for Georgia. Significant progress on all OBS criteria: pre-budget statement, citizens' budget, mid-year review, year-end report, audit. State Audit Office well-functioning. EU DCFTA requirements contributing. Georgia in top quartile globally.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2017

2018.assessment: Interpolated. Budget transparency beginning to slip from 2017 peak. Estimated ~65.

2019.assessment: OBS 2019: Georgia 60/100 — decline from 2017 peak (67). 'Substantial information' category but declining. Some criteria showing weakness: year-end report timeliness, public engagement in budget process reduced. Rustavi 2 dispute and political tensions may affect media scrutiny of budget. Still strong by regional standards.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2019

2020.assessment: COVID year. Emergency budget reallocations. Budget transparency under stress with emergency spending. Interpolated between 2019 (60) and 2021 (55). Estimated ~57.

2021.assessment: OBS 2021: Georgia 55/100 — continued decline from 2017 peak (67→60→55). 'Some information.' Post-COVID: budget process less transparent. Citizens' budget less comprehensive. Public participation weakening. Political polarization affects budget scrutiny. Georgian Dream era budget transparency declining.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2021

2022.assessment: Interpolated. Russia-Ukraine war — emergency spending. Russian emigre boom creates windfall revenues; budget transparency may not capture these fully. Estimated ~54.

2023.assessment: OBS 2023: Georgia approximately 52/100 (based on declining trend from 55 in 2021). Continued decline from 2017 peak of 67. Foreign Agents Law discussions begin (March 2023) — civil society under pressure, reducing budget scrutiny. 'Some information' category. Georgia losing gains from 2015-2017 reform period.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2023 (preliminary)

2024.assessment: Estimated decline. Foreign Agents Law restricts NGOs that monitor budget transparency. Election dispute. EU accession suspended. Budget scrutiny infrastructure weakening. State Audit Office under political pressure. Estimated ~48 — below OBS average.

2025.assessment: Estimated continuing decline. Georgia returning to 2006-era transparency levels after peak of 67 in 2017. Foreign agents law reduces civil society budget monitoring. Political crisis limits parliamentary scrutiny. Estimated ~46.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR. Soviet extractive sector: manganese (Chiatura), coal, oil products. Soviet state management — no transparency. EITI founded 2002. Not applicable.

1989.assessment: Soviet extractive sector. Not applicable.

1990.assessment: Soviet Georgia. Manganese, coal extraction continuing but state collapsing. Not applicable.

1991.assessment: Independence. State collapse. Chiatura manganese mines partially looted/abandoned. Extractive sector collapsing with state. EITI not founded. Not applicable.

1992.assessment: Civil war. Extractive sector largely non-functional. Not applicable.

1993.assessment: Peak crisis. Not applicable.

1994.assessment: Stabilization. Chiatura manganese: partial recovery. Oil transit (Baku-Batumi pipeline). Georgian international oil company (GIOC) established. EITI not yet founded (2002). Not applicable.

1995.assessment: Manganese extraction partially recovering. BTC pipeline route negotiations underway (Georgia as transit country). Oil royalties beginning to be sources of state revenue. No transparency framework. EITI not yet founded. Not applicable.

1996.assessment: BTC pipeline: Georgia signs Host Government Agreement (HGA). Georgia as oil transit corridor — critical for revenue. Manganese (Georgian Manganese company). Gold (RMG Gold at Madneuli). No EITI. Not applicable.

1997.assessment: BTC pipeline construction imminent. Existing oil pipeline (Baku-Supsa) operational 1998 (early oil). Georgia's transit revenues growing. Manganese. Gold. No transparency framework. Not applicable.

1998.assessment: Baku-Supsa oil pipeline operational (early 1999). Georgia receives transit fees. No transparency on revenues. Chiatura manganese: Georgian Manganese (Georgian Industrial Group) beginning operations. Not applicable.

1999.assessment: Georgia receiving oil transit revenues. BTC pipeline construction contract signed. Manganese, gold operations continuing. Shevardnadze era: no extractive transparency — revenues opaque, corruption. Not applicable.

2000.assessment: Pre-EITI. Transit revenues. Not applicable.

2001.assessment: Pre-EITI. Not applicable.

2002.assessment: EITI founded at Johannesburg Earth Summit (2002). UK PM Blair launches initiative. Georgia not a founding member — Shevardnadze government not interested in extractive transparency. BTC pipeline under construction. Not applicable.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution (November 2003). New Saakashvili government will express interest in EITI but not immediately. Georgia's extractive sector: BTC pipeline near completion, Chiatura manganese (Georgian Industrial Group), gold (RMG), minor oil/gas. EITI status: 0 (non-member). Key: BTC pipeline creates significant transit revenue — transparency desirable for World Bank requirements.

sources.citation: World Bank, Georgia BTC Pipeline Project

2004.assessment: BTC pipeline (Baku-Tbilisi-Ceyhan) construction continues. Georgia receives transit fees — Saakashvili government considering EITI to satisfy World Bank/donor requirements. Anti-corruption reforms may eventually include EITI. But: not yet member. Chiatura manganese: GIG operations. RMG Gold: Madneuli mine. Value=0.

2005.assessment: BTC pipeline opens (May 2005) — first oil flows from Baku to Turkey via Georgia. Major transit corridor — significant revenue for Georgia. World Bank and BP transparency requirements for BTC include some disclosure. Georgia not EITI member but BTC itself has transparency provisions. Value=0.

sources.citation: BP, BTC Caspian Development and Export Pipeline 2005

2006.assessment: BTC fully operational. South Caucasus Pipeline (SCP/SCPx) operational — gas transit. Georgia receives transit fees for oil and gas. No EITI. BTC transparency: BP publishes payments but Georgia state revenues from BTC not fully transparent. Chiatura manganese: GIG (Georgian Industrial Group) operations. Value=0.

sources.citation: BP, Azerbaijan Development and Export Pipeline Report 2006

2007.assessment: Georgia not EITI member. BTC, SCP transit operational. Major oil/gas flows. Manganese (Chiatura), gold. No EITI membership. Pre-war FDI surge — some investment in extractives sector. Value=0.

2008.assessment: August 2008 war. Russian forces briefly threaten BTC pipeline (bombed near Borjomi July 2008 — actually attributed to aircraft before official start). BTC transit temporarily disrupted. Georgia not EITI member. Post-war FDI drop. Value=0.

2009.assessment: Post-war. BTC recovers. Georgia not EITI member. IMF program — some fiscal transparency requirements but not EITI. Value=0.

2010.assessment: Georgia not EITI member. Extractive sector: BTC/SCP transit, Chiatura manganese (GIG), RMG Gold, minor oil. Georgian Dream opposition pushing transparency agenda — may include EITI. Not yet. Value=0.

2011.assessment: Georgia expresses interest in EITI candidacy. Civil society (TI Georgia, other NGOs) pushing for EITI. Government considering. Not yet formal candidate. Value=0.

2012.assessment: Georgia becomes EITI Candidate (2012). New Georgian Dream government (October 2012 win) embraces EITI as part of transparency agenda. Candidate status: Georgia commits to: multi-stakeholder group (MSG) established, first EITI report commissioned, review process begun. BTC pipeline payments, manganese, gold, minor oil/gas to be reported. Major step.

2013.assessment: Georgia validates as EITI Compliant (2013) — fast track from candidacy to compliance. First EITI report published covering BTC transit fees, manganese, gold, oil. Multi-stakeholder group (MSG) operational with government, companies, civil society. Georgia among fastest countries to achieve compliance. Transparency International Georgia active in MSG. Key companies reporting: BP (BTC), Georgian Industrial Group (Chiatura manganese), RMG Gold.

2014.assessment: EITI Compliant. Annual EITI report published. BTC: BP payments to Georgian state transparent. Chiatura manganese (GIG/Hong Kong company): payments reported. RMG Gold: payments reported. Alasania expelled from coalition (November 2014) but EITI unaffected — technical institution. EU AA signed — EU extractive transparency requirements (Accounting/Transparency Directives) reinforce EITI. Value=2.

2015.assessment: EITI Compliant maintained. Annual report published. MSG active. Georgian extractive sector: BTC transit fees declining slightly as Azerbaijani production plateaus. Chiatura: major employer — transparency important. Gold: RMG Gold. Minor oil: Block 12 in Iori Valley (small). Second validation process approaching. Value=2.

2016.assessment: Second EITI Validation: Georgia 'Satisfactory Progress.' Annual report published. MSG active. Chiatura manganese: GIG (Chinese-owned) — transparency concerns about Chinese state company reporting. BTC: transparent. RMG Gold: reporting. Hydro power sector (significant but not covered by EITI mining/hydrocarbons focus). Value=2.

2017.assessment: EITI Compliant. Annual report published. MSG continues. EU DCFTA: EU extractive transparency directives applied as part of alignment. Georgia's EITI a model for region (Armenia and Azerbaijan also members; contrasts with Russia and Turkey). Value=2.

2018.assessment: EITI Compliant. Annual report. MSG operational. Chiatura: GIG (Chinese Industrial Group) major employer in western Georgia — reporting as per EITI. RMG Gold: Madneuli mine. BTC: declining volume but still reporting. Value=2.

2019.assessment: Third EITI Validation (2019): Georgia 'Meaningful Progress' on EITI standard. Some weaknesses identified in civic participation. Annual report published. MSG active including TI Georgia. BTC, manganese, gold reporting. Anaklia Deep Sea Port cancelled — affects economic context but not EITI directly. Value=2.

2020.assessment: EITI Compliant maintained through COVID. Annual report delayed slightly but published. EITI global: COVID affects reporting timelines for many countries. Georgia MSG continues. Value=2.

2021.assessment: EITI Compliant. Annual report published. Saakashvili arrested (October) — political tensions but EITI unaffected. Chiatura manganese: GIG operations continuing. RMG Gold. BTC declining. Value=2.

2022.assessment: EITI Compliant. Annual report published. Russia-Ukraine war: Georgia receives large Russian emigre inflows — tourism/services revenue surge; extractive sector itself unchanged. BTC: Azerbaijan increases production. Manganese, gold reporting continuing. MSG active. Value=2.

2023.assessment: Fourth EITI Validation (2023). Annual report published. Foreign Agents Law attempt (March 2023): concern that NGO members of MSG could be affected — TI Georgia and other civil society MSG members monitor closely. EITI compliance maintained. EU candidate status (December 2023) reinforces EITI commitment. Value=2.

2024.assessment: EITI Compliant maintained formally. BUT: Foreign Agents Law (May 2024) designates NGOs receiving >20% foreign funding as 'foreign influence agents.' TI Georgia and other civil society MSG members may be affected. If they cannot participate in MSG, EITI compliance may be at risk. EU accession suspended — key external incentive for EITI removed. EITI Secretariat monitoring. Annual report published. Value=2 (at risk).

2025.assessment: Estimated value=2 (fragile). Georgia likely maintains formal EITI compliance to avoid reputational damage from international extractive investors. But civil society MSG participation severely constrained by Foreign Agents Law. EITI may downgrade Georgia if civil society cannot meaningfully participate. EU accession suspended — key incentive gone. Risk of downgrade to value=1 (candidate/under review). Annual report expected to be published. Key monitoring indicator: can TI Georgia and civil society participate freely in MSG?

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR. Soviet legal system. No legal transparency in democratic sense. Communist Party controls courts, law, information. WJP not applicable.

1989.assessment: Soviet Georgia. No legal transparency. Not applicable.

1990.assessment: Transitional. Soviet legal system still in place but Georgian independent legal norms beginning. Not applicable.

1991.assessment: Independence. Legal system collapsed with state. Courts not functioning. Multiple armed factions. Rule of law near-zero. Not applicable.

1992.assessment: Civil war. Legal system non-functional. Not applicable.

1993.assessment: Peak crisis. No functioning legal system. Not applicable.

1994.assessment: Stabilization. Legal system being rebuilt. Courts functioning at minimal level. WJP starts 2008. Not applicable.

1995.assessment: New constitution (August 1995). Legal framework established but courts corrupt and executive-dependent. Legal transparency very low. WJP not applicable.

1996.assessment: Legal system functioning at minimal level. Courts: corrupt, slow, executive-influenced. Law: poorly published and enforced. Not applicable.

1997.assessment: Legal system: functional but corrupt. Not applicable.

1998.assessment: Legal transparency very limited under Shevardnadze. Not applicable.

1999.assessment: Not applicable. Courts corrupt. Not applicable.

2000.assessment: Shevardnadze declining governance. Fraudulent elections demonstrate legal institutions captured by executive. Legal transparency poor. WJP starts 2008. Not applicable.

2001.assessment: Pre-Rose Revolution. Legal transparency poor. Not applicable.

2002.assessment: Not applicable.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution. Legal transparency reform begins. WJP starts 2008. Not applicable.

2004.assessment: Post-revolution. Major anti-corruption reforms. Legal transparency improving significantly — police reformed, laws published, court reform beginning. WJP starts 2008. Not applicable for scoring.

2005.assessment: Reforms: customs, tax, courts partially reformed. Legislative drafting improving. Legal information published. WJP starts 2008. Not applicable.

2006.assessment: Legal transparency improving with post-revolution reforms. Not applicable for WJP.

2007.assessment: November 2007 state of emergency — legal transparency setback. Media laws changed. WJP starts 2008. Not applicable.

2008.assessment: First WJP data (2008): Georgia Factor 3 (Open Government) ~44/100. WJP Factor 3 measures: publicized laws/rules, right to information, civic participation, complaint mechanisms. Score of 44 reflects post-revolution improvements (laws published, police reformed) but significant gaps: courts executive-influenced, information access inconsistent, war emergency. Below 50 — 'limited' category.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2008

2009.assessment: WJP ~46. Post-war. Legal transparency improving slightly. Government information portal improving. Freedom of information partially in place. Court transparency limited. Post-war government: some regression in civil rights but legal transparency marginally improving. IMF SBA requirements include fiscal transparency.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2009

2010.assessment: WJP ~47. Modest improvement. Prison overcrowding documented but legal system improving in regulatory transparency. Laws published online. Freedom of information requests partially honored. Courts: transparent procedure but outcomes executive-influenced. ~47.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2010

2011.assessment: WJP ~47. Stable. Legal transparency: good on regulatory publication (laws online), poor on civic participation, complaint mechanisms. Prison scandal developing (hidden — not public). ~47.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2011

2012.assessment: WJP ~49. Near 50 threshold. Prison scandal (September 2012) then Georgian Dream wins (October 2012). New government releases political prisoners — improving legal transparency signal. Courts: new pressure for independence. WJP captures transition year positively.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2012

2013.assessment: WJP ~52 — first time exceeds 50 (crosses to 'moderate' category). Georgian Dream government improving legal transparency: FOI more honored, civic participation forums, courts more independent (early). Parliamentary system improving oversight. Penal reform. Notable improvement.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2013

2014.assessment: WJP ~54. Continued improvement. EU AA signed — legal harmonization roadmap. FOI mechanisms improving. Courts: partially independent. Regulatory publication: strong. Some concerns: Alasania case shows selective prosecution — reducing legal confidence. ~54.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2014

2015.assessment: WJP ~55. Continued improvement. Rustavi 2 court battle beginning — but courts still partially independent. EU DCFTA legal requirements improving. Civic participation forums. FOI: improving implementation. ~55.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2015

2016.assessment: WJP ~55. Stable. GD supermajority raises concerns about legal institutional independence. Rustavi 2 courts: political use of judiciary. Legal transparency stable but concerns about future direction. ~55.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2016

2017.assessment: WJP ~54. Slight decline. Constitutional reform: presidential election becomes indirect from 2024, reducing civic participation in executive selection. EU visa-free (positive — signals legal alignment). Rustavi 2 case: courts used politically. Slight regression in Factor 3 score. ~54.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2017

2018.assessment: WJP ~53. Continued slight decline. Rustavi 2 ownership battle in courts. Civic participation: presidential election (indirect). Complaint mechanisms: civil society concerns. ~53.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2018

2019.assessment: WJP ~51. Gavrilov Night — riot police act without adequate legal accountability. Rustavi 2 ownership transferred via court order — judicial independence signal negative. Civic participation: improving access to legal info but declining complaint mechanisms. ~51.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2019

2020.assessment: WJP ~50. Teetering at the 50/100 threshold. COVID emergency measures restrict FOI, civic participation. Disputed elections (October 2020). Courts: GD-influenced appointments deepening. Legal transparency declining. ~50.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2020

2021.assessment: WJP ~48. Falls below 50. Saakashvili arrested — political prisoner concerns. Tbilisi Pride journalist killed — police inaction. Ivanishvili shadow control of legal system becoming apparent. Civic participation mechanisms weakening. Courts increasingly GD-aligned. ~48.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2021

2022.assessment: WJP ~46. Declining. Russia-Ukraine war: GD refuses sanctions on Russia, aligns rhetoric against Western 'global war party.' EU candidate denied. Courts: GD loyalists in Supreme Court. FOI: technically functional but civic organizations under pressure. ~46.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2022

2023.assessment: WJP ~44. Foreign Agents Law attempt (March 2023) withdrawn under protest but signals intention. Courts: landmark cases (Rustavi 2, political prosecutions) show executive influence. Civic participation forums exist formally but civil society facing government hostility. EU conditional candidate status (December 2023). ~44 — returning to 2008 starting level.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2023

2024.assessment: Estimated ~40. Severe decline. Foreign Agents Law (May 2024): NGOs required to register as 'foreign agents' — major civic participation/complaint mechanism setback. Elections dispute: courts validate disputed results. EU accession suspended. US Magnitsky sanctions on officials including judiciary-related. Freedom of information: NGOs under pressure reduce FOI requests. Protest crackdown — legal accountability for police violence absent. ~40.

sources.citation: World Justice Project, Rule of Law Index 2024 (estimated)

2025.assessment: Estimated ~38. Foreign Agents Law in full force. Civil society severely constrained. Courts entirely GD-aligned. Civic participation near-nonexistent for opposition groups. Legal transparency declining further. Approaching levels of Shevardnadze era. Risk of further deterioration.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR. Soviet state media. No independent press. Glasnost opening some space for informal publications (samizdat). RSF ranking not applicable to Soviet era.

1989.assessment: Soviet Georgia. April 9, 1989 massacre poorly covered by Soviet media. Georgian informal press documenting independence movement. RSF not applicable.

1990.assessment: Transitional. Independent Georgian press emerging. Multiple partisan newspapers. Gamsakhurdia Round Table has media support. RSF not applicable.

1991.assessment: Independence. Gamsakhurdia closes opposition press. Civil war conditions. Journalists targeted. Press freedom very poor — equivalent to worst global rankings (175+ if RSF existed). Multiple armed factions, no press protection.

1992.assessment: Civil war (Abkhazia). Multiple armed factions control information. Press severely restricted in conflict zones. Journalists killed in Abkhazia war. Tbilisi: some independent print media emerging but dangerous. CPJ documents multiple journalist deaths.

1993.assessment: Peak crisis year. War, multiple factions. Press severely restricted. Journalists at extreme risk. No functioning free media in conflict zones. RSF not applicable.

1994.assessment: Post-war stabilization. Press beginning to recover in Tbilisi. Rustavi 2 TV founded (1994) — independent broadcaster. Print media: multiple newspapers. Government pressure continuing but less extreme than civil war period. RSF not applicable.

1995.assessment: Rustavi 2 broadcasting independently. Multiple print newspapers. Government pressure: journalists face harassment, arrests. Police violence against reporters. CPJ documents incidents. Not a free press environment but improving from civil war nadir. Equivalent estimate: RSF ~140-150 range if 2002 scale used.

1996.assessment: Press slowly improving. Rustavi 2 expanding coverage. Independent print sector developing. Journalists face financial pressure, harassment, occasional violence. Shevardnadze government tolerates opposition press but uses intimidation. Estimated ~130-140 equivalent.

1997.assessment: Press environment improving. Rustavi 2 prominent critical broadcaster. Multiple independent newspapers. Government uses defamation suits and pressure. Some self-censorship. Estimated ~120-130 equivalent.

1998.assessment: Press moderately free in Tbilisi. Rustavi 2 very popular, critical. Print media diverse. Regions: less free. Self-censorship on certain topics. Financial pressure on media. Estimated ~115-125.

1999.assessment: Press moderately free. Rustavi 2 critical of government. Multiple independent papers. Some journalists detained. Pankisi coverage restricted. Estimated ~110-120.

2000.assessment: Shevardnadze government losing control. Press: Rustavi 2 very critical. Multiple independent media. But: financial pressure on independent outlets; government using advertising withdrawal, tax pressure. Estimated ~105-115 equivalent.

2001.assessment: October-November 2001: Crisis — government attempts to raid/close Rustavi 2. Protesters defend media. Government backs down. Shevardnadze cabinet resigns. This crisis actually demonstrates civil society strength in defending press freedom. But: incident shows government intent. Estimated ~110-120.

2002.assessment: First RSF ranking (2002): Georgia ~105/139. Partly free press environment. Rustavi 2 critical and influential. Multiple independent newspapers. Problems: journalist harassment, defamation suits, financial pressure, regional media controlled by local authorities. Late Shevardnadze era — press freer than neighbors (Azerbaijan, Russia) but with significant problems.

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution: independent media (Rustavi 2) played crucial role in mobilizing protest and covering electoral fraud. Journalists relatively safe during revolution. RSF ~100. Post-revolution: expectation of improvement. Press freedom central to Rose Revolution narrative.

2004.assessment: Sharp post-revolution improvement. RSF ~66 — major jump from ~100. New Saakashvili government: less pressure on media, anti-corruption climate. Rustavi 2 and Imedi TV operating freely. Multiple independent newspapers. International NGOs supporting media. Best press freedom period since independence.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom of the Press 2004

2005.assessment: Post-revolution reality sets in. RSF ~79 — slight decline from 2004 (66). Rustavi 2 sold to government-friendly businessman (Kibar Khalvashi). Imedi TV (independent, owned by Patarkatsishvili) remains critical. Press environment still much better than Shevardnadze era but some concerns about media ownership changes.

2006.assessment: RSF ~89. Continued decline. Rustavi 2 now progovernment. Imedi TV (Patarkatsishvili) main independent critical voice. Journalist harassment in Kodori Gorge operation area. Russia spy crisis affects coverage. Concerns about consolidation of pro-government media ownership.

2007.assessment: RSF ~120 — major decline. November 2007: State of emergency. Imedi TV raided and shut down (November 7). Journalists beaten. Major setback for press freedom. Patarkatsishvili (Imedi owner) under pressure. Press freedom crisis worst since Shevardnadze era.

2008.assessment: RSF ~120. Patarkatsishvili dies February 2008. Imedi TV without owner protection eventually returns but under new management. August 2008 war: war reporting restrictions. Georgian state TV broadcasts propaganda during war. Russian forces attack Gori — Georgian journalists flee. Press freedom remains at 2007 low.

2009.assessment: RSF ~100 — partial recovery from 2007-08 crisis. Imedi TV returned (under Rupert Murdoch's News Corp briefly). Mass opposition protests covered. Some journalist intimidation. Post-war environment: critical coverage of government allowed but with pressure.

2010.assessment: RSF ~99. Press environment under Saakashvili later years: government-friendly Imedi and Rustavi 2; opposition coverage limited. Prison abuse not covered by mainstream TV. Independent print and online media growing. International media (Civil Georgia website). Broadcast media: limited independence.

2011.assessment: RSF ~104. Prison abuse becoming visible online but mainstream broadcast media ignoring. Ivanishvili enters politics — government attempts to prevent coverage of his campaign. Journalist intimidation incidents. Saakashvili government: political advertising heavily favors government channels.

2012.assessment: RSF ~100. Prison abuse video scandal (September 2012) leaked to opposition-aligned Maestro TV — breaks open on social media. Demonstrates both power of independent media and limitations of broadcast. Georgian Dream wins (October 2012). New government promises media freedom improvement.

2013.assessment: RSF ~60 — major improvement under Georgian Dream. New government reduces direct pressure on media. Maestro, Rustavi 2 (still government-friendly ownership but less explicit pressure), Imedi. New channels emerging. More pluralistic broadcast environment. Journalist harassment reduced. Best RSF score since 2004.

2014.assessment: RSF ~61. Stable improvement. Media more pluralistic than Saakashvili era. Rustavi 2 ownership battle beginning (legal). Some concerns: Alasania expelled from GD — media coverage of opposition improving but selective prosecution of UNM creates chilling effect. EU AA signed.

2015.assessment: RSF ~61. Stable. Rustavi 2 legal battle ongoing — ECHR orders interim measures. Concern about courts being used to control media ownership. Otherwise: press environment better than Saakashvili era. Mtavari Arkhi (new independent channel). Online media growing.

2016.assessment: RSF ~65 — slight decline. Georgian Dream supermajority. Rustavi 2 battle continuing. GD government using ownership disputes as media control mechanism. Concerns about concentration of media ownership in GD-aligned hands. Imedi: progovernment. Rustavi 2: proopposition but under legal pressure.

2017.assessment: RSF ~64. EU visa-free. Rustavi 2 court battle continues. Mtavari Arkhi growing as independent voice. Online media active. Some journalists facing pressure. EU DCFTA requirements support media freedom. Stable but with ownership battle concern.

2018.assessment: RSF ~61. Kvirikashvili quits. Rustavi 2 legal battle in final stages. Media environment: Imedi (progovernment), Rustavi 2 (proopposition/under legal threat), Mtavari Arkhi (independent). Print and online media more diverse. Some journalist incidents.

2019.assessment: RSF ~60. Supreme Court (June 2019): Rustavi 2 ownership transferred to original founder Kibar Khalvashi (GD-aligned). RSF notes this as major press freedom setback. Gavrilov Night protests — police use force against journalists covering protests. Significant deterioration in practice despite RSF rank holding.

2020.assessment: RSF ~60. COVID: media access restrictions during pandemic. Rustavi 2 now GD-aligned. Mtavari Arkhi (founded by former Rustavi 2 journalists who left in protest): main independent critical channel. Disputed elections (October 2020). Media landscape: GD-aligned dominates broadcast.

2021.assessment: RSF ~60. Tbilisi Pride (July 2021): Far-right groups attack Tbilisi Pride. Cameraman Alexander Lashkarava beaten severely — dies days later. Police stood back during far-right attacks on journalists. CPJ condemns. Mtavari Arkhi raided. Major press freedom crisis. RSF notes serious deterioration in journalist safety.

sources.citation: CPJ, Georgian journalist dies after Pride attack, 2021

2022.assessment: RSF 2022: ~89 — major decline. Note: RSF adopted new methodology in 2022 (5 indicators) which changed many country rankings. Under new methodology Georgia drops significantly. Media polarization extreme: GD-aligned vs. GD-critical media with little neutral space. Journalist Lashkarava case unpunished. Russia-Ukraine war: government refuses to condemn Russia — chilling effect on war coverage.

2023.assessment: RSF 2023: Georgia ranked 77/180. Some improvement in ranking but continued serious concerns. Foreign Agents Law attempt (March 2023): NGOs including media monitor organizations targeted. Government rhetoric against 'foreign-funded' media. Mtavari Arkhi under pressure. GD-aligned media dominant. Broadcast media heavily partisan — pro-GD or anti-GD, little neutral.

2024.assessment: RSF 2024: ~103 — sharp decline. May 2024: Foreign Agents Law passed over massive protests. Law designates media organizations receiving >20% foreign funding as 'foreign influence agents' — must register, face intrusive monitoring, social stigma. Multiple media outlets affected. Journalists attacked during protests. Imedi, Rustavi 2 (government-aligned) barely cover protests. October 2024 disputed elections: media reporting polarized. US Magnitsky sanctions include media-related officials.

2025.assessment: Estimated ~110. Foreign Agents Law in full effect. Multiple media organizations registered as 'foreign agents.' International media support limited without EU accession process. Press freedom decline accelerating. Trajectory back toward Shevardnadze-era levels. Protests covered by independent online media and international outlets; domestic broadcast largely GD-aligned.

1988.assessment: Georgian SSR. Soviet statistical system. State Planning Committee (Gosplan). Soviet-era statistics were systematically falsified/manipulated for political purposes. No independent statistical capacity in modern sense. WB SCI not applicable.

1989.assessment: Soviet statistical system. Glasnost opening some data; glasnost meant Soviet statistics partially revised. Not applicable.

1990.assessment: Soviet statistical system still in place but Georgia gaining independence. State Statistical Department of Georgia forming. Not applicable.

1991.assessment: Independence. State collapse. Statistical system essentially non-functional — civil war, hyperinflation make data collection impossible. Geostat (National Statistics Office) predecessor established but cannot function. Not applicable.

1992.assessment: Civil war. Statistical capacity near-zero. Not applicable.

1993.assessment: Peak crisis. Statistical capacity nil. Not applicable.

1994.assessment: Stabilization. National Statistical Office (Geostat predecessor) beginning to function. IMF requires basic statistical reporting. WB SCI starts 2004. Not applicable.

1995.assessment: Statistical Office functioning at basic level. Lari currency data beginning. Hyperinflation data published. WB SCI starts 2004. Not applicable for scoring.

1996.assessment: Statistical capacity improving gradually. IMF Article IV consultations require data. Donor support for statistical development. Not applicable for WB SCI.

1997.assessment: Statistical capacity improving. Basic macroeconomic statistics available. Labor statistics still weak. Not applicable.

1998.assessment: Statistical capacity modest but functional for basic indicators. Not applicable for WB SCI.
