1999.assessment: Statistical capacity improving slowly under Shevardnadze. Not applicable.

2000.assessment: Statistical capacity still limited. GDP estimates unreliable due to large informal economy. Labor statistics based on household surveys. Not applicable for WB SCI.

2001.assessment: Statistical capacity modest. Not applicable for WB SCI.

2002.assessment: Not applicable for WB SCI (starts 2004).

2003.assessment: Rose Revolution. Statistical reform will be part of wider reform agenda. WB SCI starts 2004. Not applicable.

2004.assessment: First WB SCI data for Georgia (2004): approximately 57.8/100. Post-revolution reforms beginning to improve statistical infrastructure. Three pillars assessed: statistical methodology (~56), data collection (~60), data dissemination (~58). Below 60 — significant capacity gaps. Geostat (National Statistics Office) being reformed. Tax reform greatly improves fiscal data. IMF Data Standards (GDDS) compliance improving.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2005.assessment: WB SCI ~61.1. Improvement. Post-revolution reforms improving statistical capacity. Customs and tax administration data improving. Household survey (SILC equivalent) being conducted. Geostat modernizing. IMF GDDS compliance strengthening.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2006.assessment: WB SCI ~64.4. Continued improvement. Geostat reform program. Population census preparation (2002 census conducted). Labor Force Survey improving. National accounts methodology upgrading. Better than regional average.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2007.assessment: WB SCI ~67.8. Strong improvement. Geostat now reasonably functional. BTC pipeline data for trade statistics improving. National accounts rebased. IMF SDDS consideration. Pre-war year — statistical capacity at new high.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2008.assessment: WB SCI ~68.9. August 2008 war: data collection disrupted in conflict zones but core statistical systems not affected. Geostat continues functioning. Post-war reconstruction data collected. Statistical capacity resilient.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2009.assessment: WB SCI ~71.1. Continued improvement despite post-war recession. IMF SBA program requires enhanced statistical reporting. Geostat reform continuing. Data dissemination improving.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2010.assessment: WB SCI ~74.4. Geostat conducts Labor Force Survey (LFS) revision — leads to major upward revision in unemployment estimates (from ~16% to ~21%). This shows improving statistical capacity to measure previously hidden labor market realities. Population census 2014 planned.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2011.assessment: WB SCI ~76.7. Georgia approaching 80/100 threshold. Statistical methodology improving. National accounts methodology approach SNA 2008. Data dissemination: Geostat website improving. Open data initiatives.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2012.assessment: WB SCI ~78.9. Georgian Dream wins (October 2012). Statistical capacity: high and improving. Geostat: reforming governance. New government continues statistical improvement — seen as technical/non-political area.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2013.assessment: WB SCI ~80.0 — milestone: exceeds 80/100. Significant achievement for post-Soviet country. Geostat fully reformed. Open data portal. SDDS subscription. Population and Housing Census 2014 preparation in full swing. Strong statistical methodology and dissemination.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2014.assessment: WB SCI ~81.1. Population and Housing Census conducted (2014). Census data improves population statistics foundation. EU Association Agreement signed — EU statistical standards (Eurostat) becoming alignment target. Strong continued improvement.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2015.assessment: WB SCI ~82.2. Continued improvement. Post-census data integration. EU alignment on statistics. Geostat implementing Eurostat standards. National accounts rebased using 2014 census data. Strong statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2016.assessment: WB SCI ~83.3. Near peak. Geostat: highly regarded in region. DCFTA requires detailed trade statistics. Public finance statistics improving. Strong dissemination infrastructure.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2017.assessment: WB SCI ~84.4 — peak or near-peak. EU visa-free requires strong identity/border statistics. Financial sector statistics improving. Geostat: modern, open-data focused. Among top performers globally for its income level.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2018.assessment: WB SCI ~84.4. Stable at peak. Geostat strongly integrated with international standards. No signs of decline. Statistical independence maintained despite political turbulence.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2019.assessment: WB SCI ~84.4. Stable. Gavrilov Night protests do not affect statistical capacity. Rustavi 2 transfer does not affect statistical institution. Geostat independent in practice.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia

2020.assessment: WB SCI ~84.4 — final year of WB SCI (discontinued 2020). COVID: Geostat adapts data collection to pandemic conditions. Some survey limitations. Otherwise stable. WB replacing SCI with Statistical Performance Indicators (SPI) from 2021. Final SCI score for Georgia: ~84.4.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Capacity Indicator Georgia (final)

2021.assessment: WB SCI discontinued. WB now uses Statistical Performance Indicators (SPI). Georgia SPI 2021: strong performer. Estimated SCI-equivalent ~83. Geostat continues functioning well. Some concerns about political pressure on Geostat given GD consolidation, but statistical independence maintained.

sources.citation: World Bank, Statistical Performance Indicators 2021, Georgia

2022.assessment: Estimated ~82. Russia-Ukraine war: refugee/emigre data becomes important for Geostat — challenge. Russian emigre arrivals create estimation challenges. Political environment: GD refuses Russia sanctions, potential tensions with Eurostat alignment. Geostat independent in practice. Estimated slight decline.

2023.assessment: Estimated ~81. Foreign Agents Law attempt (March 2023): some concerns about NGOs that support statistical transparency. Geostat itself not directly targeted. EU candidate status requires strong statistical capacity. Geostat continuing to align with Eurostat. Estimated slight decline from post-SCI uncertainty.

2024.assessment: Estimated ~79. Foreign Agents Law passed (May 2024): NGOs supporting statistical transparency affected. EU accession suspended — removes key incentive for statistical improvement. Political crisis: some risk of Geostat independence being undermined. Data on protests, police force possibly sensitive. Overall statistical capacity still high but declining from peak. Estimated ~79.

2025.assessment: Estimated ~78. Continuing decline. Without EU accession pathway, motivation for Eurostat alignment weakens. Political crisis potentially affecting institutional independence. Still significantly above 2004 starting point (57.8) but declining from 84.4 peak.
